alignments.preprint_claim_text: Crazy-paving was identified as a major pattern of bacterial pneumonia, while Ground-glass opacities (GGOs) in the peripheral lungs were associated with COVID-19. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Deep chest CT analysis with constructed lesion clusters revealed well-known COVID-19 CT manifestations comparable to manual CT analysis. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication recasts the specific pattern associations as part of cluster-derived, well-known manifestations; the core associations are preserved but presented at cluster/manifestation level.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The constructed patient level histogram with/without radiomics features showed improved accuracy for both disease and severity classification. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The constructed histogram features improved accuracy for both diseases and severity classification. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the histogram-feature finding but presents it with higher certainty; meaning and scope are consistent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Complications such as hydrocephalus, cerebral hernia, and deep vein thrombosis, as well as brain regions (frontal, parietal, and temporal lobes), significantly contributed to post-stroke epilepsy (PSE). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Complications such as hydrocephalus, cerebral hernia, and deep vein thrombosis, as well as specific brain regions (frontal, parietal, and temporal lobes), significantly contribute to post-stroke epilepsy (PSE). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication text repeats the same set of complications and brain regions with equivalent strength and result framing, so the claim is effectively unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Factors like age, gender, NIH Stroke Scale (NIHSS) scores, and laboratory results such as WBC count and D-dimer levels were associated with a higher risk of PSE. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Factors such as age, gender, NIH Stroke Scale (NIHSS) scores, and laboratory results like WBC count and D-dimer levels are associated with increased PSE risk. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The variables and association are the same, but the publication states the association with higher reported certainty, indicating amplified confidence rather than a content change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Tree-based methods such as Random Forest, XGBoost, and LightGBM demonstrated strong predictive performance for PSE, achieving an AUC of 0.99. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Tree-based methods like Random Forest, XGBoost, and LightGBM showed strong predictive performance, achieving an AUC of 0.99. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same models and identical AUC, so the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model successfully predicts PSE risk, with tree-based models showing superior performance. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model accurately predicts PSE risk, with tree-based models demonstrating superior performance. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion that tree-based models perform best and the model predicts PSE risk is restated without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The NIHSS score, WBC count, and D-dimer were identified as the most important predictors of PSE. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: NIHSS score, WBC count, and D-dimer were identified as the most crucial predictors of PSE. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The set of top predictors and the strength of the conclusion are identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The cancer prediction model achieved a ROC Area Under the Curve (AUC) of 0.76, with an accuracy of 0.70, sensitivity of 0.67, and specificity of 0.70. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The cancer prediction model achieved a ROC Area Under the Curve (AUC) of 0.76, with an accuracy of 0.70, sensitivity of 0.67 and specificity of 0.70. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Metrics and wording are the same between preprint and publication, indicating no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The CHD prediction model achieved a higher ROC AUC of 0.87, with an accuracy of 0.83, sensitivity of 0.78, and specificity of 0.83. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The CHD prediction model achieved a higher ROC AUC of 0.87, with an accuracy of 0.83, sensitivity of 0.78 and specificity of 0.83. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The performance metrics are identical, so this result is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This study identifies key demographic and laboratory features significantly associated with cancer and CHD risk in the NHANES dataset. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This study identifies key demographic and laboratory features significantly associated with cancer and CHD risk in the NHANES dataset. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion is repeated verbatim with the same certainty in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The findings suggest that these factors could be valuable for estimating individual risk and could inform machine learning models aimed at early detection and screening. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The findings suggest that these factors could be valuable for estimating individual risk and could inform machine learning models aimed at early detection and screening. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The content of the conclusion is the same, but the publication expresses reduced confidence (medium→low), so the claim's confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The non-contact thermal imaging modality can continuously monitor hemodynamic shock in patients. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study advances a noncontact thermal imaging modality for continuous monitoring of hemodynamic shock. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Both statements present the same contribution: continuous noncontact thermal monitoring of hemodynamic shock, with matching confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Deep learning was utilized to preprocess and extract the Center-to-Peripheral Difference (CPD) in temperature values from video data. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Deep learning was used to preprocess and extract the Center-to-Peripheral Difference (CPD) in temperature values from the videos. (certainty=high, type=method)
alignments.rationale: The method description is effectively identical; only the label (contribution→method) differs but the content is the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The predictive models achieved an area under the receiver operating characteristics curve of 0.81 ± 0.06 and an area under the precision-recall curve of 0.78 ± 0.05 at 5 hours. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The models achieved the best area under the receiver operating characteristic curve of 0.81 ± 0.06 and area under the precision-recall curve of 0.78 ± 0.05 at 5 h. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Numeric results and timing are the same in both versions, indicating no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The approach provides sufficient time to stabilize patients by predicting shock status up to the next 6 hours. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The approach provides a reliable shock prediction using an automated decision pipeline that can provide better care and save lives. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication broadens and strengthens the original conclusion: instead of specifying a 6-hour stabilization window, it emphasizes reliable prediction and direct clinical impact (better care, saving lives) with higher certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The automated decision pipeline can provide better care and potentially save lives. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The approach provides a reliable shock prediction using an automated decision pipeline that can provide better care and save lives. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The core claim about better care and saving lives via the automated pipeline is retained, but the publication expresses this more strongly and with higher certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study achieved an AUC of 0.92 and an AUPR of 0.82 for myocardial infarction detection using lead-I of ECG data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study achieved an AUC of 0.92 and an AUPR of 0.82 for myocardial infarction detection using lead-I of ECG data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Metrics and claim are identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Using 6-lead and 12-lead configurations resulted in an AUC of 0.99 for myocardial infarction detection. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Using 6-lead and 12-lead configurations resulted in an AUC of 0.99 for myocardial infarction detection. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The result for multi-lead configurations is unchanged in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The findings demonstrate the potential for detecting myocardial infarction using only lead-I, as measured by wearable devices. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The findings demonstrate the potential for detecting myocardial infarction using only lead-I as measured by wearable devices. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion about lead-I wearable detection potential is restated without change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This advancement could significantly enhance clinical outcomes for myocardial infarction patients by enabling timely detection and intervention. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This advancement could significantly enhance clinical outcomes for myocardial infarction patients by enabling timely detection and intervention. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The clinical-impact conclusion is unchanged in content and expressed certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Wearable sensors can continuously monitor vital signs such as heart rate and activity, making them effective for stress monitoring. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not restate a general claim that wearables are effective for stress monitoring; it focuses on model results and methods, so this high-level contribution appears omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study proposes a machine learning-based approach for detecting the onsets of stress in a free-living environment using wearable sensors. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study emphasizes the importance of model selection and preprocessing techniques in enhancing classification performance. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents concrete model- and preprocessing-focused findings that clarify and operationalize the preprint's proposed ML approach for free-living stress detection.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN) model achieved the highest accuracy of 98% in detecting stress from the SWEET dataset. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: For binary classification with SMOTE, the K-Nearest Neighbors model performed best, with an accuracy of 95.70% and an F1-score of 95.70%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports different (lower) KNN performance under a specific preprocessing condition (with SMOTE) and does not support the preprint's unconditional 98% KNN-best claim, indicating a revised and narrowed result with tempered confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Most existing works on stress monitoring have focused on data acquired in controlled settings rather than in free-living environments. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not explicitly restate this limitation about prior work; instead it focuses on model results and processing choices, so the preprint limitation is not carried forward.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The Random Forest model demonstrated superior performance in binary classification without SMOTE, achieving an accuracy of 98.29% and an F1-score of 97.89%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This specific detailed result for Random Forest (not present in the preprint list) appears newly reported in the publication.

alignments.pub_claim_text: In the three-level classification without SMOTE, the Random Forest model excelled, achieving an accuracy of 97.98% and an F1-score of 97.22%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication includes additional model-level results for three-level classification that were not in the preprint, so these are added claims.

alignments.pub_claim_text: For three-level classification with SMOTE, XGBoost showed the highest performance, with an accuracy and F1-score of 98.98%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This explicit high-performing XGBoost result is new in the publication relative to the preprint's claim list and is therefore an added claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The median age of patients with venous thromboembolism (VTE) was significantly older than that of non-VTE individuals.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report a result about median age differences; the demographic age claim from the preprint is absent in the published claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Key predictors such as age, alcohol consumption, and specific medical conditions were significantly associated with VTE outcomes.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Key predictors of VTE risk included elevated D-dimer levels, premorbid mRS, and large vessel occlusion.
alignments.rationale: The publication lists a different set of key predictors (biomarkers and stroke features) rather than the demographic/lifestyle predictors named in the preprint, representing a substantive revision and narrowing of the predictor set.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The gradient boosting machine (GBM) model shows the highest prediction accuracy with an AUC of 0.923.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The GBM model achieved the highest predictive accuracy with an AUC of 0.923, outperforming other models such as Random Forest and Logistic Regression.
alignments.rationale: The AUC and relative performance of the GBM model are reported identically in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This study presents an innovative, machine learning-based approach to predict VTE risk in acute ischemic stroke patients.
alignments.pub_claim_text: This study provides an accurate and interpretable method to predict VTE risk in patients with AIS using the GBM model.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the core contribution (ML-based VTE risk prediction) but adds emphasis on accuracy and interpretability, clarifying the original contribution.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Future research could explore integration into clinical decision systems for broader application.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Further validation is needed to assess the broader clinical applicability of the model.
alignments.rationale: Both claims discuss moving toward clinical use; the publication frames this as a need for further validation, which clarifies and slightly reframes the preprint's suggestion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Video-based machine learning analysis can provide a more precise evaluation of dynamic facial function for patients with facial paralysis.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Video analysis provides a significantly more accurate and detailed assessment of facial movements than previously reported.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the core claim that video analysis yields more accurate/detailed assessment of facial movements, matching the preprint's conclusion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed methods can objectively assess facial nerve function by quantifying palsy severity and mapping it to widely used clinical scores.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our work allows for precise classification of facial palsy types, identification of asymmetric regions, and assessment of palsy severity.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves objective assessment of severity but expands the claim to include classification and localization, broadening and clarifying the original method-level claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Utilizing videos for analysis offers robust quantitative assessments of dynamic facial movements without the need for manual evaluation.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Video analysis provides a significantly more accurate and detailed assessment of facial movements than previously reported.
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes improved accuracy and detail from video analysis, which aligns with and clarifies the preprint's point about robust quantitative (and less manual) assessment.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The long-term potential of this project is to provide clinicians with more accurate and timely information for making decisions regarding facial reanimation surgery.
alignments.pub_claim_text: This project enables clinicians to have more accurate and timely information to make decisions for facial reanimation surgery.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the preprint's anticipated clinical impact—providing clinicians with more accurate and timely decision information for reanimation surgery.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The EfficientNet-b4 architecture used in the image grading module achieves comparable performance to the state-of-the-art for EyePACS-Q, with an F1-score of 0.86.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The EfficientNet-b4 architecture used in the image grading module achieves performance comparable to that of the state of the art for EyePACS-Q, with an F1-score of 0.86.
alignments.rationale: The result is reported identically in preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The confidence analysis reduces the number of images incorrectly assessed as gradable by 76%.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The confidence analysis reduces the number of images incorrectly assessed as gradable by 76%.
alignments.rationale: The reported effect size and claim are the same in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: AutoMorph modules perform well even when external validation data shows domain differences from training data.
alignments.pub_claim_text: AutoMorph modules perform well even when external validation data show domain differences from training data.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the conclusion about robustness to domain differences with similar wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Vascular morphology features measured from AutoMorph segmentation map and expert annotation show good to excellent agreement.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Vascular morphology features measured from the AutoMorph segmentation map and expert annotation show good to excellent agreement.
alignments.rationale: Agreement between AutoMorph and expert annotations is reported the same way in both documents.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: By making AutoMorph publicly available and open source, we hope to facilitate ophthalmic and systemic disease research.
alignments.pub_claim_text: AutoMorph has been made publicly available, facilitating widespread research in ophthalmic and systemic diseases.
alignments.rationale: The preprint framed availability as a hope; the publication states the tool is available and emphasizes facilitation of research, which strengthens (amplifies) the contribution claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: AgeDiffuse outperformed conventional DL frameworks in predicting brain age, achieving a mean absolute error (MAE) of 2.78 years in one external validation and 1.97 years in another.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The AgeDiffuse model outperformed conventional deep learning frameworks in predicting brain age, achieving a mean absolute error of 2.78 years in a multisite external validation.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the 2.78 MAE external validation result matching the preprint's first external validation result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: AgeDiffuse outperformed conventional DL frameworks in predicting brain age, achieving a mean absolute error (MAE) of 2.78 years in one external validation and 1.97 years in another.
alignments.pub_claim_text: In a separate external validation, AgeDiffuse yielded a mean absolute error of 1.97 years.
alignments.rationale: The publication separately reports the second external validation MAE of 1.97 years, which is explicitly present in the preprint's combined claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: AgeDiffuse brain age predictions reflected age-related brain structure volume changes better than biological age, with R2 values of 0.48 compared to 0.37.
alignments.pub_claim_text: AgeDiffuse brain age predictions reflected age-related brain structure volume changes better than biological age, with R² values of 0.48 compared to 0.37.
alignments.rationale: The R² comparison between predicted brain age and biological age is reported identically in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Longitudinal predicted brain age tracked closely with chronological age at the individual level.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Longitudinal predicted brain age tracked closely with chronological age at the individual level.
alignments.rationale: The longitudinal tracking claim is preserved with the same level of certainty in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The AgeDiffuse model is publicly available and usable for the research community, facilitating independent validation and application.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The AgeDiffuse model has been made publicly available for independent validation and application by the research community.
alignments.rationale: Both preprint and publication state the model is publicly available for community use and validation.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The deep-learning-based model achieved a ROC AUC of 0.903 for identifying significant coronary stenosis in the internal test set.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The deep learning model achieved a ROC AUC of 0.903 for identifying significant stenosis on the internal test set.
alignments.rationale: The internal test set AUC is reported identically in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model's performance on an external test set yielded ROC AUC values of 0.833, 0.798, and 0.780 when compared to visual assessment, 3D quantitative coronary angiography, and fractional flow reserve, respectively.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The performance of the deep learning model on the external test set yielded ROC AUC values of 0.833, 0.798, and 0.780 when compared to visual assessment, 3D quantitative coronary angiography, and fractional flow reserve, respectively.
alignments.rationale: The external test set AUCs and comparison modalities are reported the same in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The deep-learning-based approach demonstrates a performance increase compared to previous work and includes all 16 segments without excluding revascularized patients.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Compared to previous work, the approach demonstrates a performance increase and includes all 16 segments without excluding revascularized patients.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the claimed improvements over prior work and the inclusive segmentation approach.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The deep-learning model simplifies the assessment process by using fewer steps and fewer models.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report or emphasize simplification of the assessment pipeline; that specific claim about fewer steps/models is absent.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The deep-learning-based stenosis estimation models showed promising results for predicting stenosis.
alignments.rationale: The publication includes a general concluding statement about promising results for stenosis prediction that was not explicitly present as a separate claim in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Macroscopic neural circuits measured using magnetoencephalography (MEG) are indicative of the PTSD phenotype and severity. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Macroscopic neural circuits measured using magnetoencephalography (MEG) are indicative of the PTSD phenotype and severity. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The preprint and publication text are essentially identical in content and wording about MEG circuits indicating PTSD phenotype and severity.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The nested CV-SVM-rRF-FS analysis selected the minimum numbers of edges per frequency that could serve as a PTSD signature. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This specific methodological/feature-selection result appears in the preprint but is not represented in any publication claim; the publication omits these selection details.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The final SVM models built with selected features showed excellent PTSD classification performance with an area-under-curve value up to 0.9. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The final SVM models built with selected features showed excellent PTSD classification performance with an area-under-curve value up to 0.9. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The AUC result is stated identically in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: These developments provide a comprehensive machine learning-based computational framework for classifying other mental health challenges using MEG connectome profiles. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The classification model for PTSD provides a comprehensive machine learning-based computational framework for classifying other mental health challenges using MEG connectome profiles. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Both texts present the same concluding claim that the classification model/framework can be applied to other mental health challenges.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning methods can effectively reduce the number of gene/non-coding RNA feature vectors to overcome overfitting problems in the analysis of schizophrenia. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning is a powerful algorithm to reduce functional biomarkers in SCZ patients. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the preprint's conclusion in broader terms (reducing functional biomarkers), clarifying/generalizing the methodological benefit of ML for SCZ biomarker reduction.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The average predictive accuracy for schizophrenia patients was 67% based on coding genes and 96% based on long non-coding RNAs. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The average predictive accuracy for SCZ patients was 67% based on coding genes, and 96% based on long non-coding RNAs (lncRNAs). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The numeric predictive-accuracy results are stated identically in both documents.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The largest contribution factor for coding genes includes genes critical in neurodevelopment and previously associated with various brain disorders. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: This specific attribution about coding-gene contributors in neurodevelopment/brain disorders does not appear among the publication claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Genomic loci of long non-coding RNAs are enriched for genes critical in synapse function, cell junction, neuron differentiation, phosphorylation, and the Wnt signaling pathway. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The detailed enrichment result for lncRNA loci is not represented in the publication claims provided.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Long non-coding RNAs capture the characteristics of schizophrenia tissue more accurately than mRNA. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The lncRNAs capture the characteristics of SCZ tissue more accurately than mRNA. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Both versions state the same comparative conclusion about lncRNAs versus mRNA.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Combining a standard polygenic risk score as a feature in the XGBoost model increases the percentage variance explained of the prediction model compared to the standard PRS by 25% for sleep duration, 26% for height, 44% for systolic blood pressure, 64% for triglycerides, and 85% for total cholesterol. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Combining a PRS as a feature in an XGBoost model results in a relative increase in the percentage variance explained compared to the standard linear PRS model by up to 100% for diastolic blood pressure. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports different magnitude and emphasizes a different trait (diastolic BP up to 100%) rather than the specific trait-by-trait increases reported in the preprint, so the numerical/result claim was revised and focused.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning models trained in specific racial/ethnic groups performed similarly in multi-ancestry trained models, despite smaller sample sizes. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Multi-ancestry trained models perform similarly to specific racial/ethnic group trained models and are consistently superior to the standard linear PRS models. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the equivalence between multi-ancestry and group-specific models and additionally asserts superiority to linear PRS models — a clarification/expansion of the comparative performance claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The predictions of the machine learning models were superior to the standard PRS in each of the racial/ethnic groups in our study. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Multi-ancestry trained models perform similarly to specific racial/ethnic group trained models and are consistently superior to the standard linear PRS models. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reiterates superiority to standard PRS models in consistent terms; the core claim of superiority is preserved and presented more strongly ("consistently superior").

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Among Blacks, the percentage variance explained for total cholesterol was substantially lower than for other groups, with values of 8.1%, 12.9%, and 17.4% for Blacks, Whites, and Hispanics/Latinos, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication claims do not include this specific subgroup numerical breakdown for total cholesterol.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This work demonstrates an effective method to account for non-linearities and interaction effects in genetics-based prediction models. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This work demonstrates an effective method to account for non-linearities and interaction effects in genetics-based prediction models. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion about addressing non-linearities and interactions is stated identically in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Heritability of the continuous notch smoothness trait was 7.5%, compared with 5.6% for the binary absent notch trait. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Heritability of the continuous notch smoothness trait was 7.5%, compared with 5.6% for the binary absent notch trait. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The heritability estimates are identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A genome wide association study of notch smoothness identified 15 significant loci, implicating genes including NT5C2, IGFBP3, and PHACTR1. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A genome-wide association study of notch smoothness identified 15 significant loci, implicating genes including NT5C2, IGFBP3, and PHACTR1. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The GWAS locus discovery claim is the same in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Notch smoothness stratified risk of incident myocardial infarction or coronary artery disease, stroke, heart failure, and aortic stenosis. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Notch smoothness stratified risk of incident myocardial infarction or coronary artery disease, stroke, heart failure, and aortic stenosis. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The risk-stratification result is preserved verbatim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A polygenic risk score for notch smoothness was associated with incident CVD and all-cause death in UK Biobank participants without available PPG data. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Raw digital phenotyping may identify individuals at risk for disease via specific genetic pathways. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the specific PRS association as a broader implication about raw digital phenotyping identifying at-risk individuals via genetic pathways, shifting from a specific association result to a generalized conclusion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Greater notch smoothness was associated with greater risk of incident CVD. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Greater notch smoothness was associated with greater risk of incident cardiovascular disease. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion about greater notch smoothness increasing CVD risk is consistent and unchanged between versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The BASH-GN machine learning based questionnaire outperformed four commonly used OSA screening questionnaires in predicting obstructive sleep apnea risk. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The BASH-GN, a machine learning–based questionnaire, outperformed four commonly used OSA screening questionnaires in predicting obstructive sleep apnea risk. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The performance claim is reproduced verbatim in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The BASH-GN achieved an AUROC of 0.78 on the SHHS 1 test set and 0.76 on the WSC set. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The BASH-GN achieved an area under the receiver operating characteristic (AUROC) of 0.78 on the SHHS 1 test set and 0.76 on the WSC set. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The reported AUROC values and datasets match exactly between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Considering OSA subtypes when evaluating OSA risk can improve the accuracy of OSA screening. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Considering OSA subtypes when evaluating OSA risk may improve the accuracy of OSA screening. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same conceptual recommendation but softens wording ("can" -> "may") and lowers stated certainty, so the claim is clarified and confidence tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A decrease in activity and sleep efficiency can be observed in depressed patients. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A decrease in activity and sleep efficiency can be observed in depressed patients. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication claim is textually identical and conveys the same result and certainty as the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Physiological changes, such as heart rate variability, can be used to distinguish depressed patients from normal persons. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Physiological changes, such as heart rate variability, can be used to distinguish depressed patients from normal persons. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The claim is the same in publication and preprint with no substantive change in meaning or certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed research will explore and construct machine learning models capable of detecting depressive episodes and assessing their severity using data collected from wristband-type wearable devices. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed research will explore and construct machine learning models capable of detecting depressive episodes and assessing their severity using data collected from wristband-type wearable devices. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The contribution statement is reproduced unchanged in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our machine learning model could improve the clinical diagnosis and management of depression through the use of a wearable medical device. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our machine learning model could improve the clinical diagnosis and management of depression through the use of a wearable medical device. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion about potential clinical improvement via a wearable device appears verbatim and unchanged in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The interactive active machine-learning model training methodology achieved a macro-accuracy of 94.7% for death certificate records. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The machine-learning model achieved a macro-accuracy of 94.7% for classifying death certificate records. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The numerical result and context are the same between preprint and publication with only minor wording differences.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The interactive active machine-learning model training methodology achieved a macro-accuracy of 92.4% for aeromedical retrieval episodes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The machine-learning model achieved a macro-accuracy of 92.4% for classifying aeromedical retrieval episodes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The claim is equivalent in content and certainty; wording is slightly adjusted but meaning is preserved.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The time taken to develop and train the algorithm was 124 minutes for death certificate coding and 144 minutes for aeromedical retrieval coding. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The time taken to develop and train the algorithm was 124 minutes for death certificate coding and 144 minutes for aeromedical retrieval coding. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The timing results are identical between versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This machine-learning training method was able to classify free-text clinical notes quickly and accurately into categories relevant to clinicians. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This machine-learning training method was able to classify free-text clinical notes quickly and accurately. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication shortens the phrasing (drops the explicit phrase "into categories relevant to clinicians") but preserves the conclusion that the method classifies free-text quickly and accurately.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The optimized GMAC showed better detection performance than the previously proposed GMAC. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The optimized GMAC showed better detection performance than the previous GMAC and comparable performance to the best-performing machine learning-based measure. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the original comparison to the previous GMAC but adds that performance is also comparable to the best ML-based measure, expanding the claim's scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The optimized GMAC had comparable performance to that of the best-performing machine learning-based measure (random forest inter-subject model). (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The optimized GMAC showed better detection performance than the previous GMAC and comparable performance to the best-performing machine learning-based measure. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents the comparable-performance point but omits the specific "random forest inter-subject model" phrasing and places it within a combined statement; the result is retained but expression is generalized.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The optimized GMAC is simpler and well suited for real-time upper-limb use detection. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The optimized GMAC can be a valuable measure for offline or real-time detection and feedback of upper limb use. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication keeps the real-time utility claim but broadens the applicability to offline use and feedback; wording and certainty are slightly adjusted but the conclusion remains.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The optimized GMAC is the best option when a trained machine learning-based intra-subject model or labeled data is unavailable. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: No publication claim asserts that optimized GMAC is explicitly "the best option" when intra-subject models or labeled data are unavailable; that specific recommendation was not retained.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The optimized limb-specific GMAC measure had better performance than the generic measure. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This specific comparison (limb-specific vs generic GMAC) appears in the publication but was not present in the preprint.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The preliminary results of this study need to be validated on a larger dataset to evaluate its generalizability. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The call for larger-scale validation is introduced in the publication and was not present in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Presence of diarrhea and nausea was significantly higher in the emergency department group than in the COVID-19 outpatient test center. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The presence of diarrhea and nausea was significantly higher in the emergency department group than in the COVID-19 outpatient test center. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The result is reproduced verbatim with identical meaning and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Among liver enzymes, only aspartate transaminase (AST) has been significantly elevated in the hospitalized group compared to patients discharged home. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Among liver enzymes, only aspartate transaminase (AST) has been significantly elevated in the hospitalized group compared to patients discharged home. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same statistically significant AST elevation finding without change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Using the random forest algorithm, elevated AST has been identified as the most important predictor for COVID-19 related hospitalizations. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Random Forest algorithm identified AST as the most important predictor for COVID-19 related hospitalizations, followed by age and diabetes mellitus. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves AST as the top predictor but adds that age and diabetes mellitus are subsequent important predictors, expanding the predictive context.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SARS-CoV-2 positivity is connected with isolated AST elevation and the level is linked with the severity of the disease. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: SARS-CoV-2 positivity is connected with isolated AST elevation, and the level is linked with the severity of the disease. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The claim's content is the same, but the publication presents it with higher certainty (certainty level increased), so confidence is amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Diarrhea and bloating have predictive importance for COVID-19 hospitalizations, although much lower than AST. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report the specific claim that diarrhea and bloating have predictive importance for hospitalizations (relative to AST), so this preprint claim was not retained.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The deep learning model effectively recognized the healthy and injured meniscus with an overall average precision of more than 88%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The deep learning model effectively recognized healthy and injured menisci with an average precision ranging from 68% to 80%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports substantially lower average-precision values than the preprint; the recognition claim remains but the reported performance is revised downward, reducing confidence in the earlier numeric claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The diagnosis accuracy for the healthy, torn, and degenerated meniscus was 87.50%, 86.96%, and 84.78%, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Diagnostic accuracy for healthy, torn, and degenerated menisci was 87.50%, 86.96%, and 84.78%, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The diagnostic accuracy percentages are identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Mask R-CNN has great potential in the application for the diagnosis of meniscus injury. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Mask R-CNN effectively identified and diagnosed meniscal injuries, particularly tears occurring in different parts of the meniscus. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication strengthens and specifies the conclusion: from potential applicability to effective identification/diagnosis and increased certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The diagnostic accuracy can be further improved with the increase of the training sample size. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The explicit recommendation that increasing training sample size would improve accuracy does not appear in the publication's claims, so this specific suggestion was not retained.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This technology could serve as an effective tool for clinical MRI-assisted diagnostics in meniscus injury. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The deep learning model showed great potential in diagnosing meniscus injuries and could reduce doctors' workload while improving diagnostic accuracy. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the claim of clinical utility and expands it by noting potential workload reduction and diagnostic improvement, broadening the practical implications.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Validation results from the external dataset demonstrated that the accuracy of diagnosing torn and intact meniscus tear through 3.0T MRI images was higher than 80%. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This external validation result is reported in the publication but was not present in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The advanced AI algorithm-implemented LAMP (ai-LAMP) assay showed high analytical sensitivity and specificity for SARS-CoV-2. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The advanced AI algorithm-implemented LAMP (ai-LAMP) assay showed high analytical sensitivity and specificity for SARS-CoV-2. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The preprint and publication texts are identical in content and certainty, indicating no change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The ai-LAMP assay was shown to be reliable, highly specific, and significantly more sensitive than the current gold standard qRT-PCR. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The ai-LAMP assay was shown to be reliable, highly specific, and significantly more sensitive than the current gold standard qRT-PCR. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Sentence and certainty are the same in preprint and publication, so the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The system could provide an efficient and cost-effective platform to detect SARS-CoV-2 in resource-limited laboratories. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This system could provide an efficient and cost-effective platform to detect SARS-CoV-2 in resource-limited laboratories. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion is carried forward verbatim with the same level of certainty.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The developed diagnostic device includes automated image acquisition and processing algorithms to reduce assay run time and subjectivity. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: This device-level contribution does not appear in the preprint and is newly reported in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: 60% of physicians perceived the CDSS to be a useful tool in their treatment-selection process. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: 60% of physicians perceived the CDSS to be a useful tool in their treatment-selection process. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Exact wording and certainty preserved between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: 50% of physicians would use the tool for all patients with depression. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: 50% of physicians would use the tool for all patients with depression. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The claim is identical in content and certainty in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Clinicians found the tool to be useful in discussing treatment options with patients. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Clinicians found the tool to be useful in discussing treatment options with patients. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The textual claim is the same but the publication reports a higher certainty, indicating increased confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The efficacy of this CDSS and its potential to improve treatment outcomes must be further evaluated in clinical trials. (certainty=high, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The efficacy of this CDSS and its potential to improve treatment outcomes must be further evaluated in clinical trials. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The recommendation/limitation language is unchanged but reported certainty is reduced in the publication.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Family physicians perceived the greatest utility from the CDSS. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This subgroup-specific finding does not appear in the preprint and is newly reported in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The overall accuracy of AI models for diagnosing laryngeal lesions was very high, ranging from 0.806 to 0.997. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The overall accuracy of AI models for diagnosing laryngeal lesions was very high, ranging from 0.806 to 0.997. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Identical quantitative accuracy range reported in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The pooled sensitivity and specificity for identifying healthy laryngeal tissue were 0.91 and 0.97, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The pooled sensitivity and specificity for identification of healthy laryngeal tissue were 0.91 and 0.97, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Same pooled performance metrics are reported in both documents.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: AI demonstrates extraordinarily high accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity in assessing images of laryngeal lesions. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: AI demonstrates extraordinarily high accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity in assessing images of laryngeal lesions. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Summary conclusion is preserved verbatim between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The performance of AI diagnoses increases with the size of the image database. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: This specific observation about dataset-size effects is present in the preprint but not reflected in the publication claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Multicentre cooperation should focus on creating and sharing a large database of laryngeal lesion images to improve AI model performance. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The recommended multicentre contribution for database creation appears in the preprint but is not present among the publication claims.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The pooled sensitivity and specificity for differentiation between benign and malignant lesions were 0.91 and 0.94, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This specific pooled result (benign vs malignant differentiation) is newly reported in the publication and not in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The AI model achieved an accuracy of 0.855, precision of 0.873, sensitivity of 0.827, specificity of 0.882, f1 score of 0.850, and receiver-operating-characteristics area-under-curve of 0.929. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The AI model achieved an accuracy of 0.855 in predicting elevated BNP levels from chest radiograms. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports only the accuracy and application (BNP prediction), omitting other metrics and thus narrowing and revising the original multifaceted performance claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The accuracy of the testing with AI assistance significantly improved from 0.708±0.049 to 0.829±0.069 (P < 0.001). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The accuracy of testing by healthcare professionals significantly improved from 0.708 to 0.829 with AI assistance. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The numerical improvement is the same though the publication omits error bars and p-value; the claim is clarified/simplified but substantively consistent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: With AI assistance, the accuracy of non-experts was higher than that of experts (0.851±0.074 vs. 0.803±0.054, P = 0.033). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: With AI assistance, early-career professionals had a higher accuracy (0.851) compared to veterans (0.803). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The comparison between less-experienced and more-experienced clinicians is preserved with equivalent numeric values; wording adjusted but meaning unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The AI model can predict elevated BNP levels from chest radiograms and has the potential to improve human performance. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The AI model has the potential to improve human performance in predicting elevated BNP levels. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Conclusion about potential for improving human performance is carried forward with the same focus and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Current state-of-the-art LLMs do not accurately diagnose patients across all pathologies, performing significantly worse than physicians on average. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Current state-of-the-art LLMs do not accurately diagnose patients across all pathologies, performing significantly worse than physicians. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Core comparative diagnostic performance claim is unchanged between versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: LLMs do not follow diagnostic or treatment guidelines and cannot interpret laboratory results, posing a serious risk to patient health. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: LLMs do not follow diagnostic or treatment guidelines and cannot interpret laboratory results, posing a serious risk to patient health. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The safety-related limitations are reported identically in both documents.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: LLMs cannot be easily integrated into existing workflows because they often fail to follow instructions and are sensitive to both the quantity and order of information. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: LLMs cannot be easily integrated into existing workflows because they often fail to follow instructions and are sensitive to both the quantity and order of information. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Integration and instruction-following limitations are preserved verbatim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: LLMs are currently not ready for clinical deployment. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: LLMs are currently not ready for autonomous clinical decision-making. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication narrows the conclusion from general clinical deployment to the narrower case of autonomous decision-making, changing the scope while keeping the overall caution.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A curated dataset and framework have been created to guide future studies on LLMs in clinical settings. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A curated dataset and framework have been created to guide future studies on LLMs in clinical decision-making. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The contribution is preserved but the target is narrowed from general clinical settings to clinical decision-making specifically.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The introduction of large language models (LLMs) into clinical practice promises to improve patient education and empowerment.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Most studies investigate Generative Pre-trained Transformers (GPT)-3.5 and GPT-4 in answering medical questions and generating patient information.
alignments.rationale: The preprint asserts a general promise to improve patient education/empowerment, while the publication reports that most studies focus on GPT models answering medical questions/generating patient information — shifting from a prospective benefit claim to an observational description of the literature and reducing certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This systematic review synthesizes current applications and limitations of LLMs in patient care.
alignments.pub_claim_text: This review provides a foundational framework and taxonomy for the implementation and evaluation of LLMs in healthcare settings.
alignments.rationale: The preprint's general contribution claim is retained but the publication frames the synthesis specifically as producing a framework/taxonomy, clarifying the review's output.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The analysis identifies two primary domains of LLM limitations: design and output.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The analysis identifies two primary domains of limitations for LLMs: design and output.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same two-domain finding with equivalent wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Design limitations of LLMs include lack of medical domain optimization, data transparency, and accessibility issues.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Design limitations of LLMs include lack of medical domain optimization, data transparency, and accessibility issues.
alignments.rationale: The design limitations are stated identically in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This study provides a foundational framework and taxonomy for the implementation and evaluation of LLMs in healthcare settings.
alignments.pub_claim_text: This review provides a foundational framework and taxonomy for the implementation and evaluation of LLMs in healthcare settings.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same concluding contribution with equivalent certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: 22 out of 24 studies (91.7%) identified biases in large language models (LLMs).
alignments.pub_claim_text: The review identified that 91.7% of the studies evaluated found biases in large language models.
alignments.rationale: The published result repeats the same numeric finding and interpretation.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Gender bias was the most prevalent, reported in 15 of 16 studies (93.7%).
alignments.pub_claim_text: Gender bias was the most prevalent type of bias, reported in 93.7% of the studies that examined it.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same prevalence and conclusion about gender bias.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Racial or ethnic biases were observed in 10 of 11 studies (90.9%).
alignments.pub_claim_text: Racial or ethnic biases were observed in 90.9% of the studies that assessed these factors.
alignments.rationale: The published claim is the same numeric observation and interpretation.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Mitigation strategies for biases in LLMs mainly included prompt engineering, with varying effectiveness.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Mitigation strategies for biases in large language models mainly included prompt engineering, which showed varying effectiveness.
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same mitigation finding and nuance about variable effectiveness.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There is a potential publication bias, as studies with negative results are less frequently published.
alignments.pub_claim_text: There is a potential publication bias, as studies with negative results are less frequently published.
alignments.rationale: The limitation about potential publication bias is repeated unchanged in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Sentence embeddings fine-tuned using biomedical corpora produce clusters with the highest alignment to CT-level information.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Sentence embeddings fine-tuned using biomedical corpora produce clusters with the highest alignment to CT-level information.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same experimental result about fine-tuned embeddings and alignment to CT-level information.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Human expert evaluation confirms that clusters are well-structured and coherent.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Human expert evaluation confirms that clusters are well structured and coherent.
alignments.rationale: The expert evaluation finding is reported identically in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Clusters retain significant CT information, up to 97% of the classification performance obtained with raw embeddings.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Clusters retain significant CT information, up to 97% of the classification performance obtained with raw embeddings.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same quantitative retention result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Eligibility sections automatically generated using clusters achieve 95% of the ROUGE scores obtained with a generative LLM.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Eligibility sections automatically generated using clusters achieve 95% of the ROUGE scores obtained with a generative LLM prompted with CT-protocol details.
alignments.rationale: The publication clarifies that the generative LLM was prompted with CT-protocol details, adding methodological specificity without changing the numeric result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Clustering-based approaches provide a scalable enhancement in CT design that balances information compression with accuracy.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Clustering eligibility criteria using LLM embeddings provides a practical and efficient method to summarize critical protocol information.
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the conclusion toward practicality and efficiency in summarization; this clarifies the applied benefit while retaining the core positive assessment.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: AI-enabled retinal vasculometry (RV) provides high predictive test performance for circulatory mortality.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Inclusion of artificial intelligence-enabled retinal vasculometry improves existing risk algorithms for incident stroke, myocardial infarction, and circulatory mortality.
alignments.rationale: The preprint claimed high predictive performance for circulatory mortality, while the publication frames the result as RV improving existing risk algorithms across multiple outcomes; this broadens the claim but the publication expresses lower certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The addition of RV to Framingham risk scores (FRS) did not improve model performance for incident stroke and myocardial infarction (MI) in either cohort.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Inclusion of artificial intelligence-enabled retinal vasculometry improves existing risk algorithms for incident stroke, myocardial infarction, and circulatory mortality.
alignments.rationale: The preprint reported no improvement when adding RV to FRS for stroke/MI, but the publication asserts that inclusion improves algorithms for those outcomes — a substantive revision and a broader, but more uncertain, claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The simpler RV model performed equally or better than the Framingham risk scores for predicting incident stroke and MI.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The simpler retinal vasculometry model performed equally or better than the Framingham risk scores for predicting incident stroke and myocardial infarction.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same result about the simpler RV model matching or exceeding FRS performance for stroke and MI.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: RV offers a non-invasive alternative predictive biomarker for vascular health that does not require blood sampling or blood pressure measurement.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Retinal vasculometry offers an alternative predictive biomarker to traditional risk scores for vascular health.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the alternative-biomarker conclusion but omits the explicit non-invasive details, narrowing the scope of the claim to the biomarker role without procedural specifics.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Further research is needed to examine the use of RV in population screening to identify individuals at high risk.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Further work is needed to examine retinal vasculometry in population screening to triage individuals at high-risk.
alignments.rationale: The recommendation for further research on population screening is preserved with equivalent intent and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We identified 14 key clinical features that reached a good predictive performance of AUC 0.907.
alignments.pub_claim_text: We identified 14 key clinical features whose combination reached a good predictive performance of area under the receiver operating characteristic curve 0.907.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the numeric AUC result and the same set of features.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: These key features were successfully validated in a large independent cohort containing 15,790 patients.
alignments.pub_claim_text: We successfully validated these key features in a large independent cohort containing 15,790 patients.
alignments.rationale: Validation in the same external cohort with identical patient count is reported unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our study shows that 14 key features are robust and useful in predicting the risk of death in patients confirmed SARS-CoV-2 infection at an early stage.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our study shows that 14 key features are robust and useful in predicting the risk of death in patients confirmed SARS-CoV-2 infection at an early stage.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same conclusion about robustness and early-stage predictive usefulness.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The machine learning model developed could potentially be useful in clinical settings to help in making clinical decisions.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The identified features are potentially useful in clinical settings to help in making clinical decisions.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the claim about potential clinical utility, reframing slightly from the model to the features but retaining the same implication and uncertainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Deep learning models can detect anomalies in MRI brain scans of recently diagnosed and untreated patients with Parkinson’s disease.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Deep learning models can detect anomalies in brain diffusion tensor imaging parameter maps of de novo Parkinson's disease patients.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the core result (anomaly detection in newly diagnosed PD) but specifies the imaging modality (DTI parameter maps rather than generic MRI), narrowing the scope while keeping the claim's confidence level.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Reconstruction errors computed by the models allowed classification of controls and patients with ROC AUC up to 0.81.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The most discriminating brain macro-region for detecting anomalies in de novo PD patients is the white matter, with a ROC-AUC of 68.3.
alignments.rationale: The publication replaces the single high AUC value with lower, region-specific AUCs (e.g., white matter 68.3), changing the quantitative result and narrowing it to particular brain regions; overall confidence is tempered by lower performance numbers.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The white matter and subcortical structures, particularly the substantia nigra, are the regions most impacted by Parkinson’s disease.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The best subcortical structure for anomaly detection in de novo PD patients is the GPi, with a ROC-AUC of 62.6.
alignments.rationale: Both versions identify subcortical structures as important, but the specific top structure differs (substantia nigra vs GPi) and the publication gives a region-specific performance metric, so the claim is revised while remaining at the same level of specificity (subcortical structure).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Deep learning-based anomaly detection models are promising tools for extracting robust neuroimaging biomarkers of Parkinson’s disease.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Further testing on larger cohorts is needed to validate the findings and enable the extraction of neuroimaging biomarkers of Parkinson's disease.
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the promise more cautiously by explicitly calling for further validation on larger cohorts, which clarifies and tempers the original optimistic conclusion about biomarker extraction.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Deep learning models can be extended with additional quantitative MRI parameters to provide new knowledge about the physio-pathology of neuro-degenerative diseases.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not include or restate this forward-looking claim about extending models with additional quantitative MRI parameters, so it appears to have been removed from the final paper.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The application of spatial light interference microscopy (SLIM) reduces human bias in tissue examination.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The application of spatial light interference microscopy (SLIM) reduces human bias in imaging data.
alignments.rationale: The core conclusion that SLIM reduces human bias is preserved verbatim between preprint and publication, with only minor wording changes.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Mask R-CNN deep learning algorithm applied to SLIM data achieved 91% accuracy for gland detection.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The mask R-CNN deep learning algorithm applied to SLIM data achieved 91% accuracy for gland detection.
alignments.rationale: The result (91% gland detection accuracy using Mask R-CNN on SLIM data) is identical in preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The SLIM method allows for standardized imaging conditions, improving the comparability of image data across instruments and clinics.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not explicitly state the claim about standardizing imaging conditions and comparability across instruments/clinics, so this preprint claim appears to be omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The automated colorectal cancer screening procedure achieved 99.71% accuracy in gland-level classification.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The automated colorectal cancer screening procedure classified normal vs. cancerous specimens with 99.71% accuracy in gland-level classification.
alignments.rationale: The high-accuracy gland-level classification result (99.71%) is maintained exactly in the publication, with only slight rephrasing.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A SLIM tissue scanner combined with a deep learning algorithm may enhance the speed and accuracy of pathological assessments.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A SLIM tissue scanner combined with a deep learning algorithm may enhance the speed and accuracy of pathologist assessments.
alignments.rationale: The contribution claim about improving speed and accuracy of pathology assessments is carried over unchanged into the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The deep learning method described in this study can analyze the impact of sequence variants on enhancers in pancreatic islets.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our deep learning method learns islet-specific transcription factor regulatory patterns and can prioritize candidate causal variants.
alignments.rationale: The publication articulates the method's capabilities more specifically (learning TF regulatory patterns and prioritization), which clarifies the preprint's broader statement about analyzing variant impacts.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our model successfully learns islet-specific transcription factor regulatory patterns and prioritizes candidate causal variants for type 2 diabetes.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our deep learning method learns islet-specific transcription factor regulatory patterns and can prioritize candidate causal variants.
alignments.rationale: This preprint result matches the publication text closely about learning islet-specific TF patterns and prioritizing candidate causal variants.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: For 101 genetic signals associated with type 2 diabetes, our method nominates a single causal variant for each association signal.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The method nominates a single causal variant for each of the 101 genetic signals associated with T2D and related glycemic traits.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the preprint's specific result (single nominated causal variant per 101 signals) without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Biochemical testing shows allelic effects on transcription factor binding for the model-prioritized variant associated with blood glucose levels.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Biochemical evidence of allelic effects on transcription factor binding was shown for the model-prioritized variant in a pancreatic islet beta cell line.
alignments.rationale: The publication provides additional experimental context (performed in a beta cell line) while preserving the core biochemical evidence claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model and islet enhancer perturbation scores will be publicly distributed to aid future research.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model and islet enhancer perturbation scores across ~67 million genetic variants are publicly distributed to aid future research.
alignments.rationale: The publication confirms public distribution and quantifies the scope (~67 million variants), which clarifies and broadens the original distribution claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Users in the NMPDU group express more negative emotions and less positive emotions compared to a control group.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Users in the NMPDU group express more negative emotions and less positive emotions compared to a control group.
alignments.rationale: The emotional valence finding is identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: NMPDU posts tend to be highly polarized, indicating potential emotional triggers.
alignments.pub_claim_text: NMPDU posts tend to be highly polarized, indicating potential emotional triggers.
alignments.rationale: The polarization result is preserved verbatim in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Female users in the NMPDU group express more content related to positive emotions and concerns about family, friends, home, health, and the past, and less about anger compared to males.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Female users in the NMPDU group express more content related to positive emotions and concerns about family, friends, home, health, and the past compared to male users.
alignments.rationale: The gender-differentiated content patterns are retained, but the publication reports this with higher certainty (certainty increased), so confidence is amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The findings of the study can enrich our understanding of NMPDU.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The findings can enrich our understanding of NMPDU and the population involved.
alignments.rationale: The interpretive/conclusion claim is kept and slightly expanded to mention 'the population involved'; the publication also reports this with higher certainty, so confidence is amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Field studies demonstrated high levels of sensitivity (100%) and specificity (94.4%, CI 92.8-96.1%) for reading IgG band of COVID-19 antibodies RDTs compared to visual readings from health workers.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Field studies demonstrated high levels of sensitivity (100%) and specificity (94.4%) for reading IgG band of COVID-19 antibody RDTs compared to visual readings from health workers.
alignments.rationale: The field-study performance metrics for IgG band reading are preserved in the publication with essentially identical values.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Sensitivity of detecting IgM test bands was 100% and specificity was 95.8%, CI 94.3-97.3%.
