alignments.pub_claim_text: The associations identified are broadly consistent with expectations based on prior knowledge from health specialists. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The agreement with domain expectations appears unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The results, and the ability to attribute confidence and explanations, support the wider adoption of AI in healthcare. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The results, and the ability to attribute confidence and explanations, support the wider adoption of AI in healthcare. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The broader conclusion advocating AI adoption is repeated without change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Natural language inference (NLI) methods can be applied to convert registry curation into an NLI problem. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A single pretrained NLI model can curate multiple registry fields simultaneously, unlike traditional natural language processing methods that require specially annotated training sets. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the original idea more concretely (single pretrained model curating multiple fields), clarifying the application of NLI to registry curation.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: One model, ALBERT, outperforms other models on 22 out of 43 breast oncology registry fields. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The ALBERT model outperformed other NLI models on 22 out of 43 breast oncology registry fields. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The performance result for ALBERT is restated identically.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The NLI models show considerable variation in performance, both within and across registry fields. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Natural language inference (NLI) models showed considerable variation in performance when applied to clinical, laboratory, and pathology notes for breast oncology registry curation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds context (specific note types) and reports the observation with stronger certainty, so the claim is clarified and its reported confidence is amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Incorrect inferences primarily arise from models’ misinterpretations of temporality and confusion based on terminology. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Incorrect inferences from NLI models primarily arose from misinterpretation of historical findings and confusion with abbreviations and subtle term variants in clinical text. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication gives more specific examples (historical findings, abbreviations/term variants) of the preprint's temporality and terminology errors, clarifying the limitation.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Modern NLI methods show promise for increasing the efficiency of registry curation, even when used 'out of the box'. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Modern NLI models could increase the efficiency of registry curation even when applied without additional training. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the promise but softens the certainty (high -> medium) and uses more cautious phrasing ('could'), so the claim is clarified and confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The publicly available GLIDE model has reasonably strong representations of key topics in cancer research and oncology. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The publicly available GLIDE model has reasonably strong representations of key topics in cancer research and oncology. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same result about GLIDE's oncology topic representations.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: GLIDE seems to lack useful representations of the style and content of radiology data. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: GLIDE seems to lack useful representations of the style and content of radiology data. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The observation about radiology representation limitations is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Domain-agnostic generative AI models can learn relevant medical concepts without explicit training. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Domain-agnostic generative AI models can learn relevant medical concepts without explicit training. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion about domain-agnostic models learning medical concepts remains the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: GLIDE and similar models might be useful for medical image processing tasks in the future with additional domain-specific fine-tuning. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: GLIDE and similar models might be useful for medical image processing tasks in the future with additional domain-specific fine-tuning. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The forward-looking claim about potential utility with fine-tuning is preserved.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DR-GPT reaches 0.975 quadratic weighted Cohen’s kappa using truncated Early Treatment Diabetic Retinopathy Study scale. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: DR-GPT reaches 0.975 quadratic weighted Cohen's kappa using truncated Early Treatment Diabetic Retinopathy Study scale. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The wording and reported metric are identical between preprint and publication, indicating no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Pairing DR-GPT annotations for unlabeled data with corresponding fundus images improves image classifier performance with statistical significance. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Pairing DR-GPT annotations for unlabeled data with corresponding fundus images improves image classifier performance with statistical significance. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The claim about improved classifier performance is carried over verbatim with the same level of certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Large language models can be applied for unstructured medical report databases to classify diabetic retinopathy with a variety of applications. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Large language models can be applied for unstructured medical report databases to classify diabetic retinopathy. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication drops the phrase 'with a variety of applications,' tightening the statement; the core conclusion remains but its suggested breadth is narrowed.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The linear model with textual and follower network features performed best with a precision of 57% at 24% recall. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The linear model with textual and follower network features achieved a precision of 57% at a recall of 24%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Metrics and model description are the same between versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: At least every other college-attending Twitter user flagged by our model is expected to be a Juul user. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: At least every other college-attending Twitter user flagged by the model is expected to be a Juul user. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion is restated with equivalent wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Social network features tend to have a large positive impact on predictive performance. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Social network features have a large positive impact on classification performance for identifying Juul users. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes but preserves the same finding about the strong positive impact of social network features.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There are enough predictive signals from social feeds for supervised modeling of Juul use, even with limited training data. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: There are enough latent signals from social feeds to support supervised modeling of Juul use with limited training data. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Content and implication are equivalent; wording slightly adjusted but meaning preserved.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This initial success indicates potential for more involved automated surveillance of Juul use based on social media data. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study indicates potential for automated surveillance of Juul use based on social media data. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same cautious conclusion about surveillance potential with only minor wording changes.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: CLAIMS trained only with MP2RAGEx4 achieved a CL true positive rate of 74% with a false positive rate of 30%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: CLAIMS trained only with MP2RAGE×4 achieved results comparable to those of the multi-contrast model, reaching a CL true positive rate of 74% with a false positive rate of 30%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The numerical results are identical, but the publication adds a comparison to the multi-contrast model, clarifying the context of the performance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Detection rate was excellent for leukocortical and subpial lesions at 83% and 70%, respectively, while it reached 53% for intracortical lesions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Detection rate was excellent for leukocortical and subpial lesions (83%, and 70%, respectively), whereas it reached 53% for intracortical lesions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The metrics and lesion-wise performance statements are the same between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: CLAIMS outperformed MSLAST when considering a minimum lesion volume of 6μL, with a lesion-wise detection rate of 71% compared to 48%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Applying a domain-scanner adaptation approach and testing CLAIMS on the second dataset, the performance was superior to MSLAST when considering a minimum lesion volume of 6 μL (lesion-wise detection rate of 71% versus 48%). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The core performance comparison is preserved but the publication adds methodological/contextual detail about domain-scanner adaptation and testing dataset.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed framework outperforms previous state-of-the-art methods for automated CL detection across scanners and protocols. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed framework outperforms previous state-of-the-art methods for automated CL detection across scanners and protocols. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion about outperforming prior methods is restated identically.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: CLAIMS may be useful to support clinical decisions at 7T MRI, especially in the field of diagnosis and differential diagnosis of multiple sclerosis patients. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: In the future, CLAIMS may be useful to support clinical decisions at 7 T MRI, especially in the field of diagnosis and differential diagnosis of MS patients. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the same cautionary conclusion, adding an explicit temporal qualifier ('In the future') but not materially changing the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The developed deep-learning-based pipeline achieved an overall AUC of 0.886 for detecting organ abnormalities in the external test cohort. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The developed deep-learning-based pipeline achieved an overall AUC of 0.886 for detecting organ abnormalities in the external test cohort. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Identical reported performance and phrasing between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Models trained on larger datasets with labels auto-extracted via the information extraction schema significantly outperformed those trained on human-annotated labels. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Models trained on larger datasets with labels auto-extracted via the information extraction schema significantly outperformed those trained on human-annotated labels. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The result is carried forward verbatim with the same certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Using disease information from routine clinical free-text radiology reports allows for the development of accurate anomaly detection models without requiring manual annotations. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Using disease information from routine clinical free-text radiology reports allows for the development of accurate anomaly detection models without requiring manual annotations. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion is unchanged in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The approach developed in this study is applicable to various anatomical sites and could streamline diagnostic processes. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The approach developed in this study is applicable to various anatomical sites and could streamline diagnostic processes. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The applicability claim is preserved without change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The developed MELD FCD surface-based algorithm had a sensitivity of 67% and a specificity of 54% on the withheld test cohort. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Across the entire withheld test cohort, the sensitivity of the algorithm was 59% and specificity was 54%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports a lower overall sensitivity (59%) for the entire withheld cohort and clarifies that 67% sensitivity applies when including a border zone, so the preprint's 67% figure for the cohort is revised and the claim is effectively narrowed and tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The algorithm achieved a sensitivity of 85% on a restricted subcohort of seizure-free patients with FCD type IIB who had T1 and FLAIR MRI data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The MELD FCD surface-based algorithm achieved a sensitivity of 85% on a restricted 'gold-standard' subcohort of seizure-free patients with FCD type IIB. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The metric and subcohort description match; publication adds 'gold-standard' qualifier but does not change the reported result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This multicentre, multinational study has developed a robust and interpretable machine-learning algorithm for automated detection of focal cortical dysplasias. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study developed a robust and interpretable machine-learning algorithm for automated detection of focal cortical dysplasias. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same overall claim about developing a robust, interpretable algorithm; differences are stylistic (contribution vs conclusion) rather than substantive.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The algorithm generates individual patient reports that highlight predicted lesion locations, alongside the imaging features and their relative saliency to the classifier. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not include or repeat the specific claim about generating individual patient reports, indicating this contribution was omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: 150 users reported on Twitter that they received at least one dose of Covid-19 vaccination during pregnancy or the periconception period.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study identified 150 users who reported receiving at least one dose of COVID-19 vaccination during pregnancy or the periconception period.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same numeric result and population without substantive change in meaning or scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Among the 60 completed pregnancies, at least one reported outcome was manually verified for 45 (75%) of them.
alignments.pub_claim_text: At least one reported outcome was manually verified for 45 of the 60 completed pregnancies, indicating a 75% verification rate.
alignments.rationale: The publication reproduces the same verification count and percentage, preserving meaning and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Twitter can be a complementary resource for potentially increasing the acceptance of Covid-19 vaccination in pregnant populations.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Twitter can serve as a complementary resource for increasing the acceptance of COVID-19 vaccination in pregnant populations.
alignments.rationale: The publication expresses the same conclusion more directly (removing 'potentially'), a small wording clarification without changing scope or expressed certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study highlights the need for more safety data on Covid-19 vaccination during pregnancy.
alignments.rationale: The publication's listed claims do not include an explicit statement about needing more safety data, so this preprint limitation was omitted from the publication claims.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Twitter data can be used to identify a cohort for epidemiologic studies of COVID-19 vaccination in pregnancy.
alignments.rationale: This publication claim (about cohort identification via Twitter) does not appear among the preprint claims and is a new stated contribution.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Of the 202 clinical vignettes evaluated for medical accuracy, 118 scored 13 points or above, indicating they were sufficiently beneficial and immediately usable with minor revisions.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Of the 202 clinical vignettes evaluated for medical accuracy, 118 scored 13 points or above, indicating they were sufficiently beneficial and immediately usable with minor revisions.
alignments.rationale: The result is identical between preprint and publication with no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: 142 vignettes scored 13 points or above for Japanese-language accuracy, demonstrating a high level of grammatical correctness.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Regarding Japanese-language accuracy, 142 vignettes scored 13 points or above, demonstrating a high level of grammatical correctness.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same metric and interpretation without change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Overall, 97% of the clinical vignettes proved practically useful based on confirmation and revision by Japanese medical physicians.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Overall, 97% of the clinical vignettes proved practically useful based on confirmation and revision by Japanese medical physicians.
alignments.rationale: Identical practical-use result reproduced in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The use of GPT is expected to greatly optimize the process of creating clinical vignettes, which traditionally requires significant effort from physicians.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Using GPT for generating clinical vignettes is expected to greatly optimize the process compared to the significant effort required by physicians to create vignettes without AI.
alignments.rationale: The conclusion about GPT optimizing vignette creation is maintained with equivalent framing in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning models achieved an average C statistic of 0.70 for predicting autism spectrum disorder (ASD) in a population-based study.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The average performance of the machine learning models had a C-statistic of 0.709, indicating moderate predictive ability.
alignments.rationale: The publication provides a more precise C-statistic (0.709 vs 0.70) but conveys the same finding about moderate predictive ability.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The sensitivity of the machine learning models for predicting ASD was 28.63%, while the specificity was 98.62%.
alignments.rationale: The publication's claims do not reproduce these specific sensitivity/specificity values, so this exact metric claim was omitted from the listed publication claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The use of electronic medical records (EMR) can enhance the ability for accurate and efficient early detection of ASD in large populations.
alignments.pub_claim_text: These approaches may enhance the ability for accurate and efficient early detection of ASD in large populations of children.
alignments.rationale: The publication expresses the same conclusion about EMR/ML approaches enhancing early detection in large populations without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The machine learning classifier developed in this study serves as a proof-of-principle for the potential utility of EMR in identifying children at high risk for ASD.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning models applied to electronic medical records can predict autism spectrum disorder (ASD) early in life.
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the proof-of-principle as an observed ability to predict ASD early; this is a slightly more direct phrasing of the preprint's conclusion.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The machine learning algorithms revealed previously unknown features associated with ASD risk.
alignments.rationale: The publication includes a claim about newly identified features associated with ASD risk that was not present in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: 3D-CNN models outperformed their 2D counterparts, with the X3D model achieving superior performance across all metrics.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The 3D-CNN models outperformed their 2D counterparts, with the X3D-L model achieving superior performance across all metrics.
alignments.rationale: The publication refines the model designation to X3D-L but conveys the same overall finding that 3D-CNN (X3D variant) outperformed 2D models.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The X3D model had lower computational demand and parameter count than 2D models.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The X3D-L model had lower computational demand and parameter count than 2D models.
alignments.rationale: The computational-efficiency claim is retained; the publication specifies the X3D-L variant, a refinement of the preprint wording.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The developed method for automating the selection of coronary angiogram video clips using 3D-CNNs potentially improves model accuracy and efficiency in clinical applications.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A rapid and effective method for automating the selection of coronary angiogram video clips using 3D-CNNs was developed.
alignments.rationale: The publication states the method was developed and characterizes it as rapid/effective, which is a stronger (amplified) and clearer framing than the preprint's tentative 'potentially improves' wording.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: When validating models on the independent dataset, slight decreases in AUC and sensitivity were observed but accuracy and specificity remained robust.
alignments.rationale: The publication's listed claims do not mention the specific validation-observed decreases in AUC/sensitivity, so this validation-detail claim from the preprint is not present among publication claims.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The X3D-L model reports a balanced trade-off between computational efficiency and complexity, making it suitable for real-life clinical applications.
alignments.rationale: The publication includes an additional claim about a balanced trade-off and suitability for clinical use that does not appear in the preprint claim list.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The screening CNN achieved excellent accuracy (98%), sensitivity (98%), and specificity (98%) in predicting a patient compared to controls.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The screening CNN achieved 99% accuracy and specificity and 98% sensitivity in predicting a patient compared with controls.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports slightly different numeric values (99% vs 98%) but conveys the same result; this is a numeric clarification.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Joint-specific CNN accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity were highest for the wrist, followed by the IFPIP and MFPIP.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Joint-specific CNN accuracy, sensitivity, specificity and AUC for the wrist were 75%, 92%, 72% and 0.86, respectively.
alignments.rationale: The publication provides specific performance metrics for the wrist (a refinement) rather than the ranking across multiple joints present in the preprint, effectively narrowing the reported detail to the wrist.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Computer vision without feature engineering can distinguish between patients and controls based on smartphone photographs with good accuracy.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Computer vision distinguishes patients and controls using smartphone photographs, showing promise as a screening tool.
alignments.rationale: The publication tones the claim toward 'showing promise as a screening tool' rather than asserting 'good accuracy', which is a clarification with somewhat tempered confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Future research includes validating findings in diverse populations and refining models to improve specificity in joints.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Future research will focus on validating findings in diverse populations and other joints and integrating this technology into clinical workflows.
alignments.rationale: The publication maintains the same validation goal but expands the future work to include other joints and clinical integration, broadening the scope of planned research.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: All models outperform the baseline constant incidence model, reducing the root mean square error (RMSE) by 23%-29% and weighted interval score (WIS) by 25%-31%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: All models outperform the baseline constant incidence model, reducing the root mean square error (RMSE) by 23%–29% and weighted interval score (WIS) by 25%–31% for 8-week ahead forecasts. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same performance ranges but adds the specific horizon (“8-week ahead forecasts”), clarifying and narrowing the claim to a forecast horizon.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The adaptive weight blending ensemble (AWBE) reduces RMSE by 45% and WIS by 46%, and outperforms individual models for forecasts of epidemic trends during both winter and summer seasons. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The adaptive weight blending ensemble (AWBE) model reduces RMSE by 52% and WIS by 53%, outperforming individual models for forecasts in different epidemic trends and during both winter and summer seasons. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication asserts the same qualitative finding but reports larger effect sizes (52%/53% vs 45%/46%), so the claim is revised with amplified effect estimates.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Using the post-COVID surveillance data in 2023-2024, the AWBE ensemble still reduces RMSE by 32% and WIS by 36%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Using the post-COVID data (2023–2024) as another test period, the AWBE model still reduces RMSE by 39% and WIS by 45%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication evaluates the same period but reports larger reductions (39%/45% vs 32%/36%), so the quantitative claim is revised and amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The framework contributes to the ensemble forecasting of infectious diseases with irregular seasonality. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our framework contributes to comparing and benchmarking models in ensemble forecasts, enhancing evidence for synthesizing multiple models in disease forecasting for geographies with irregular influenza seasonality. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication refines the contribution by emphasizing comparing/benchmarking and focuses on geographies with irregular influenza seasonality, clarifying and narrowing the original broader infectious-disease claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The adaptive weight ensemble approach dynamically updates individual model contributions and halves the RMSE compared to individual models. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The adaptive weight blending ensemble (AWBE) model reduces RMSE by 52% and WIS by 53%, outperforming individual models for forecasts in different epidemic trends and during both winter and summer seasons. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both claims state the ensemble dynamically weights models and substantially lowers RMSE relative to individuals; the publication gives a specific reduction (52%) rather than the informal “halves”, so the numeric claim is revised.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our deep learning transformer-based approach for predicting progression of cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma (cSCC) patients achieved an accuracy of AUROC=0.82. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We reached AUROC = 0.82 across all cohorts after training the model in a federated manner. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same AUROC but adds that this was achieved across cohorts with federated training, clarifying the context of the reported performance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The predictive power of our model surpasses that of clinico-pathological parameters used to assess progression risk. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Image-based risk scores achieved hazard ratios up to 7.42 (p < 0.01) in multivariable analyses. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports strong image-based risk associations (hazard ratios) but does not explicitly state a direct superiority comparison to clinico-pathological parameters, so the original comparative claim is tempered/revised.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Information located at the tumor boundaries is predictive of patient progression in cSCC. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Image-based risk scores achieved hazard ratios up to 7.42 (p < 0.01) in multivariable analyses. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports strong image-based predictive signals but does not explicitly single out tumor-boundary information; the claim is provisionally clarified by broader image-based performance metrics.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Heterogeneity of tissue morphology and organization are characteristic of progressive cSCCs. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The published claims do not explicitly state that tissue morphological heterogeneity characterizes progressive cSCCs, so this specific conclusion is not represented in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our model can be deployed and expanded across other clinical centers, offering a potentially powerful tool for improved screening and better clinical management of cSCC patients. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our model can improve secondary prevention and understanding of cSCC while enabling deployment across clinical centers without administrative overheads or privacy concerns. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication makes the same deployment claim and further clarifies practical benefits (no administrative or privacy overheads), refining the original deployment/contribution statement.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Predicting management labels directly is more accurate than predicting the diagnosis and then inferring the management decision. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Predicting management labels directly is more accurate than predicting the diagnosis and then inferring the management decision. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same finding with equivalent wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Directly predicting management decisions reduces the over-excision rate compared to management decisions inferred from diagnosis predictions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Directly predicting management decisions considerably reduces the over-excision rate compared to management decisions inferred from diagnosis predictions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same result (reduced over-excision) with similar emphasis ('considerably'), so the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Training a model to simultaneously predict the seven-point criteria and the diagnosis of skin lesions yields higher accuracy in management predictions. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Training a model to simultaneously predict the seven-point criteria and the diagnosis of skin lesions yields higher accuracy in management predictions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the same claim but reports it with higher certainty, so the substantive claim is unchanged while confidence is amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model demonstrates generalizability by agreeing with the clinical management recommendations of dermatologists. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our model agrees with the clinical management recommendations of 157 dermatologists as much as they agree amongst each other. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication provides a specific benchmark (agreement with 157 dermatologists and inter-dermatologist agreement), clarifying generalizability and strengthening the confidence in that agreement.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This review is the first to report diagnostic machine learning applications using functional near-infrared spectroscopy. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This review is the first to report diagnostic machine learning applications using functional near-infrared spectroscopy. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same statement of novelty unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There has been an increasing trend to perform machine learning applications on fNIRS-based biomarker research since 2010. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: There has been an increasing trend to perform machine learning applications on fNIRS-based biomarker research since 2010. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same trend observation with no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There is a significant negative correlation between sample size greater than 20 and accuracy values. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: There is a significant negative correlation between sample size (greater than 21) and accuracy values. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same negative correlation but slightly adjusts the sample-size threshold (greater than 21), a minor clarification of the numeric cutoff.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Support vector machine and deep learning approaches are the most popular classifier approaches in the studies evaluated. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Support vector machine and deep learning approaches are the most popular classifier approaches in the evaluated studies. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same methodological popularity finding unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Using machine learning on fNIRS data might be a promising approach to reveal specific biomarkers for diagnostic classification. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Using machine learning on fNIRS data might be a promising approach to reveal specific biomarkers for diagnostic classification. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the same tentative conclusion about promise for biomarker discovery.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The deep learning index for the AS continuum (DLi-ASc) increases with worsening AS severity and demonstrates excellent discrimination for any AS, significant AS, and severe AS. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The deep learning index for the AS continuum (DLi-ASc) increased with worsening AS severity and demonstrated excellent discrimination for any AS, significant AS, and severe AS. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same performance and discrimination characteristics unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DLi-ASc is an independent predictor for composite endpoints including cardiovascular death, heart failure, and aortic valve replacement. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: DLi-ASc was an independent predictor for composite endpoints including cardiovascular death, heart failure, and aortic valve replacement. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication affirms the same prognostic independence and endpoints unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The automatic measurement of conventional AS parameters shows high accuracy for AS staging and offers prognostic value comparable to manually-derived parameters. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Automatic measurement of conventional AS parameters demonstrated excellent correlation with manual measurement, resulting in high accuracy for AS staging. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes excellent correlation with manual measures leading to high staging accuracy, clarifying the mechanism behind the comparable prognostic value.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The AI-based system provides accurate and prognostically valuable AS assessment suitable for various clinical settings. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The AI-based system provides accurate and prognostically valuable AS assessment, suitable for various clinical settings. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same conclusion about clinical suitability and prognostic value.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Further validation studies are planned to confirm the effectiveness of the AI-based system across diverse environments. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The published claims do not include mention of planned future validation studies, so this forward-looking limitation statement is not present in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The AI system shortlisted a larger number of eligible patients with greater precision compared to the EHR search. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The AI system shortlisted a larger number of eligible patients with greater precision compared to the traditional EHR search. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Wording and meaning are effectively identical, describing the same comparative result between AI and EHR search.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A combined AI-EHR approach identified 604 eligible patients with a positive predictive value of 86%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A combined AI-EHR approach identified 604 eligible patients with a positive predictive value of 86%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the exact numeric result and framing of the combined approach.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The AI system demonstrates the potential for facilitating automated pre-screening for clinical trials in geographic atrophy. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This study demonstrates the potential for AI in facilitating automated prescreening for clinical trials in GA. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion is restated in the publication with equivalent scope and confidence about AI's potential for prescreening in GA.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The AI adjusts to the distinct imaging criteria from several clinical trials, generating tailored shortlists of eligible patients. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: No publication claim corresponds to the system adapting to multiple trial-specific imaging criteria or producing tailored shortlists; this claim is not represented in the published claims.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The positive predictive value of the AI approach in identifying trial-eligible patients was 63%, significantly higher than the 40% from the EHR search. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This publication provides an additional quantitative comparison of AI alone versus EHR not present among the preprint claims.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The intraclass correlation of GA area segmented on FAF versus AI-segmented area on OCT was 0.77 for cases meeting trial criteria. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This specific segmentation agreement metric is new in the publication and was not claimed in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The neural network classifier demonstrated a sensitivity of 74% in detecting lesions in patients with radiologically-defined lesions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Classifier sensitivity was 74% in patients with radiologically defined lesions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The sensitivity figure and patient population are the same in preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The classifier achieved a specificity of 100% as no clusters were detected in the healthy control group. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: No specificity/healthy-control claim appears among the publication claims, so this specific result was omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There was a co-localisation of 62% between classifier output and seizure onset zone contacts in patients with focal cortical seizure onset zones. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: There was colocalization between classifier output and seizure onset zone contacts in 62% of patients with histopathologically heterogeneous focal cortical lesions. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The core 62% colocalization result is preserved but the publication clarifies the histopathological heterogeneity of the cohort and reports a lower certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The algorithm successfully detected 86% of histopathologically-confirmed focal cortical dysplasias. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The algorithm correctly detected seven of eight focal cortical dysplasias, resulting in an 86% detection rate. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same detection count and percentage for histopathologically confirmed FCDs.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study created a framework for incorporating deep-learning based MRI lesion detection into sEEG implantation planning. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study created a framework for incorporating deep-learning-based MRI lesion detection into stereoelectroencephalography implantation planning. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The framework claim is present in both versions but the publication presents it with higher stated certainty.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The findings support the prospective evaluation of automated MRI analysis to plan optimal electrode trajectories. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: This forward-looking recommendation appears in the publication but was not explicitly claimed in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our model enables accurate detection of FOG manifestations with an 11.43% higher F1 score than the second-best model. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our model achieved an F1 score of 0.78 and segment F1@50 of 0.75 in detecting FOG manifestations. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication provides absolute performance metrics (F1 and segment F1) clarifying the model's performance that in the preprint was expressed as a relative improvement.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Assessment of FOG manifestation severity was moderately strong for trembling in place and strong for complete akinesia. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Assessment of FOG severity was strong for trembling (ICC=0.86) and moderately strong for akinesia (ICC=0.78). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The relative strength labels for trembling vs akinesia are reversed in the publication and numeric ICCs are provided, representing a substantive revision of the original claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Complete akinesia can be distinguished from volitional stopping. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our model successfully differentiated FOG from other forms of movement cessation during 360-degree turning-in-place tasks. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication frames this as a successful differentiation but specifies the task context (360-degree turning), narrowing the scope from the more general preprint statement.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: FOG manifestations could be accurately detected and assessed with deep learning. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our study demonstrates that DL can accurately assess different types of FOG manifestations. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The overall conclusion that deep learning can accurately detect and assess FOG manifestations is preserved.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Future work should establish whether these results hold firm for a more extensive and varied verification cohort. (certainty=low, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The specific limitation calling for larger/varied validation cohorts is not reflected among the publication claims provided.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: VinDr-CXR demonstrated a comparable performance to experienced radiologists in classifying 6 common thoracic diseases with a mean area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUROC) of 0.967. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: VinDr-CXR demonstrated a comparable performance to experienced radiologists in classifying 6 common thoracic diseases with a mean area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUROC) of 0.967. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The performance claim and numeric AUROC are identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The sensitivity of VinDr-CXR for the localization task with 14 types of lesions was 80.2% at the rate of 1.0 false-positive lesion identified per scan. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The VinDr-CXR achieved a sensitivity of 80.2% for the localization task with 14 types of lesions at the rate of 1.0 false-positive lesion identified per scan. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This localization sensitivity claim is restated verbatim in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed system significantly improved the agreement between radiologists themselves with an increase of 1.5% in mean Fleiss’ Kappa. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed system significantly improved the agreement between radiologists with an increase of 1.5% in mean Fleiss’ Kappa when used as a diagnosis supporting tool. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication clarifies the context (as a diagnostic support tool) in which the Fleiss' Kappa improvement was observed, without changing the measured effect.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: After consulting VinDr-CXR’s suggestions, the agreement between each radiologist and the system increased by 3.3% in mean Cohen’s Kappa. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: After consulting VinDr-CXR’s suggestions, the agreement between each radiologist and the system increased by 3.3% in mean Cohen’s Kappa. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The Cohen's Kappa improvement is presented identically in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The results highlight the potential of the proposed deep learning system as an effective assistant to radiologists in clinical practice. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: No explicit concluding claim about potential as an assistant appears among the provided publication claims, so this higher-level conclusion was not included.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed deep learning algorithms can accurately calculate height-adjusted total kidney volume (HtTKV) and predict chronic kidney disease (CKD) stages >=3A, >=3B, and a 30% decline in eGFR after 8 years. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The automated kidney volume segmentation model accurately calculates height-adjusted total kidney volume (HtTKV). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication separates segmentation accuracy (HtTKV calculation) from predictive modeling; the segmentation component matches but the predictive part is described elsewhere, so the original combined claim is narrowed/clarified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The algorithms achieve AUC scores of 0.96, 0.96, and 0.95 for predicting CKD stages >=3A, >=3B, and a 30% decline in eGFR, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed deep-learning algorithms achieved AUC scores of 0.96, 0.96, and 0.95 for predicting CKD stages >=3A, >=3B, and a 30% decline in eGFR, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same AUC results and predictive claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The algorithm demonstrates a Pearson correlation coefficient of 0.81 between predicted and measured eGFR decline. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The algorithm achieved a Pearson correlation coefficient of 0.81 between predicted and measured eGFR decline. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The Pearson correlation result is identical in preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Incorporating automatically generated features of kidney MRI images can improve the prognosis of renal function decline in ADPKD patients. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The combination of segmented kidney volumes, predicted HtTKV, age, and baseline eGFR can predict chronic kidney disease (CKD) stages >=3A, >=3B, and a 30% decline in eGFR after 8 years. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication operationalizes the preprint's conceptual claim by specifying which automatically generated features (volumes and predicted HtTKV) were used alongside clinical variables to make predictions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed approach might improve monitoring and support the prognosis of ADPKD patients from the earliest disease stages. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed approach has the potential to enhance monitoring and facilitate prognosis in ADPKD patients, even in the early disease stages. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same conclusion about potential to enhance monitoring and prognosis, including early-stage applicability.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Support for the development of healthcare artificial intelligence was not higher amongst socioeconomically privileged groups. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Indicators of social advantage do not predict support for healthcare AI. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same finding about healthcare AI support not being higher among socially advantaged groups with equivalent certainty and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Males, individuals with computer science experience, and younger people are more likely to support healthcare artificial intelligence. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Variables associated with support for healthcare AI include being male, having computer science or programming experience, and being aged between 18 and 34 years. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same sociodemographic predictors but with reduced stated certainty (high → medium), so content is unchanged but confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: General support for the development of artificial intelligence tends to be higher amongst more socioeconomically privileged groups. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Support for AI in general is predicted by indicators of social advantage. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication expresses the same general association between social advantage and AI support with similar certainty and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Understanding how public attitudes differ amongst sociodemographic subgroups is important for future governance of healthcare AI. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not include a claim about the importance of understanding subgroup attitudes for governance, so this preprint claim is omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Eleven out of fourteen eligible studies developed prognostic or diagnostic AI predictive models that were assessed to be at high risk of bias. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: All AI predictive models assessed were found to be at high risk of bias. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same conclusion about assessed AI predictive models being at high risk of bias; wording is slightly reorganized but the substantive claim is the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Common pitfalls in the studies included inadequate sample sizes, poor handling of missing data, failure to account for censored participants, and weak validation of models. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not enumerate these specific methodological pitfalls, so the detailed list from the preprint is not present in the published claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Current AI applications for COVID-19 are not ready for deployment in acute care settings due to their limited scope and poor quality. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Current AI applications for COVID-19 are not ready for deployment in acute care settings due to their limited scope and poor quality. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same conclusion about readiness for deployment with identical reasoning and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There is a need for improvements in AI applications to facilitate safe and effective clinical adoption, both for and beyond the COVID-19 pandemic. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: There is a need for improvements to facilitate safe and effective clinical adoption of AI applications for COVID-19. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication keeps the call for improvements but restricts the scope to COVID-19 applications (narrowing the original broader framing); the substantive recommendation remains.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The automated multi-modality pipeline for cardiac amyloidosis detection demonstrates excellent accuracy with C-statistics of 0.85-0.92 for the ECG model and 0.91-1.00 for the echocardiography model. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The C-statistics for CA detection are 0.85–0.91 for ECG and 0.89–1.00 for echocardiography. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both report high C-statistics but the numerical ranges differ between preprint and publication, indicating a revision of reported performance metrics.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The echocardiography model outperforms expert diagnosis in detecting cardiac amyloidosis. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The published claims do not state that the echocardiography model outperforms experts, so this specific comparative claim is omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Pre-screening with ECG enhances the echocardiography model performance significantly, increasing the positive predictive value from 23% and 20% to 58% and 53% at 64% recall for AMC2 and AMC3, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Pre-screening with ECG improves the performance of the echocardiography model, increasing the positive predictive value from 33% to 74–77%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both claim ECG pre-screening improves echocardiography PPV, but the specific PPV numbers differ substantially between preprint and publication, so the result is revised.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The developed pipeline for cardiac amyloidosis detection could serve as a generalizable strategy for other rare and intermediate frequency cardiac diseases. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The automated strategy developed for CA detection is expected to be generalizable to other rare cardiac diseases. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same expectation of generalizability to other rare cardiac diseases with similar certainty and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Artificial neural networks (ANNs) were used for the prediction of functional outcomes and mortality in neurorehabilitation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Artificial neural networks (ANNs) were used for the prediction of functional outcomes and mortality in neurorehabilitation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same descriptive result with no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Most ANN-based models outperformed regression or other machine learning models in neurorehabilitation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Most ANN-based models outperformed regression or other machine learning models in neurorehabilitation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The claim about ANN models outperforming alternatives is preserved unchanged in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The scoping review provides encouraging evidence to use ANNs for clinical decision-making of complex datasets in neurorehabilitation. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This scoping review provides encouraging evidence to use ANNs for clinical decision-making of complex datasets in neurorehabilitation. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same tentative conclusion about ANNs' potential for clinical decision-making.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: More research is needed to establish the clinical utility of ANNs in diagnosing, monitoring, and rehabilitation of individuals with neurological conditions. (certainty=high, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: More research is needed to establish the clinical utility of ANNs in diagnosing, monitoring, and rehabilitation of individuals with neurological conditions. (certainty=high, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The limitation calling for further research is identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Initial tests have demonstrated varying degrees of potential for implementation of AI and machine learning-assisted tools in information retrieval and evidence review processes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The exploratory tests conducted by the working group demonstrated varying degrees of potential for the implementation of artificial intelligence and machine-learning-assisted tools. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the exploratory test results about varying potential for AI/ML-assisted tools with equivalent scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The working group's approach contributes to broader discussions on ethical considerations, copyright and licensing issues, and transparency of AI methodology. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The work contributed to broader discussions on ethical considerations, copyright and licensing issues, and the transparency of artificial intelligence methodology. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the contribution claim about engaging broader discussions on ethics, copyright, and transparency without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The insights gained from navigating the AI landscape have potential implications for knowledge and library services within a public health organization. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The published claims focus on testing and broader discussion outcomes and do not mention implications specifically for knowledge and library services, so this preprint conclusion is omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our algorithm can classify brain-damaged patients vs. healthy individuals with an accuracy of 86±5%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our top-performing machine learning models classified neglect patients vs. healthy individuals with an Area Under the ROC curve (AUC) ranging from 0.83 to 0.86. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both claims report model discrimination between patients and controls but the metric changed from accuracy (±SE) to AUC; this is a substantive methodological/metric revision while the overall classification claim remains.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The algorithm scores correlate with the degree of severity of neglect signs estimated with standardized paper-and-pencil tests. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The 1D convolutional neural network scores correlated with the degree of severity of neglect behavior as estimated with standardized paper-and-pencil tests. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same correlation between model scores and standardized neglect severity measures with no substantive change in meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study introduces a new classification method to analyze eye trajectories in patients with neglect syndrome. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study introduces new methods for both the pre-processing and the classification of eye-movement trajectories in patients with neglect syndrome. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication expands the contribution by explicitly adding preprocessing methods alongside classification, clarifying and broadening the original methodological claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Deep CNNs can efficiently and non-invasively characterize left spatial neglect. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed methods can likely be applied to other types of neurological diseases opening the possibility of new computer-aided, precise, sensitive and non-invasive diagnostic tools. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The preprint's specific strong claim about CNNs characterizing left spatial neglect is reformulated in the publication as a more tentative, broader statement about potential applications and diagnostics, reducing confidence and shifting scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We find a clear correlation with the microstructure of the third branch of the superior longitudinal fasciculus using Diffusion Tensor Imaging (DTI). (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The integrity of white matter tracts measured from Diffusion Tensor Imaging (DTI) showed a clear correlation with the third branch of the superior longitudinal fasciculus (SLF), especially damaged in neglect. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both statements report the same DTI finding linking microstructure/integrity of the third SLF branch to neglect, with equivalent emphasis and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: ChatGPT performed at or near the passing threshold for all three exams of the USMLE. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: ChatGPT performed at or near the passing threshold for all three exams of the USMLE. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this result verbatim with no change in meaning or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: ChatGPT demonstrated a high level of concordance and insight in its explanations. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: ChatGPT demonstrated a high level of concordance and insight in its explanations. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Identical claim and certainty in preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Large language models may have the potential to assist with medical education. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Large language models may have the potential to assist with medical education. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion about potential to assist medical education is unchanged between versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Large language models may have the potential to assist with clinical decision-making. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Large language models may have the potential to assist with clinical decision-making. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same conclusion regarding potential assistance in clinical decision-making.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Synthetic data is an emerging alternative to address the challenges of accessing high-quality data in healthcare. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Synthetic data technologies hold significant promise for future applications despite their early stage. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Both claims convey the same high-level conclusion that synthetic data is a promising/emerging solution for healthcare data access, with only slight wording differences.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A total of 13 main healthcare domains were identified where synthetic data are currently generated, with Oncology, Neurology, and Cardiology being the most frequently mentioned. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Thirteen main healthcare domains were identified where synthetic data are currently generated, with Oncology, Neurology, and Cardiology being the most frequently mentioned. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this enumerative result exactly.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The predominant type of data generated in the context of synthetic data is unstructured, particularly images. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Unstructured data, particularly images, were found to be the predominant type of synthetic data generated. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Same finding restated with equivalent emphasis and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Five types of motivations and three principal future uses for synthetic data in healthcare were identified. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Five primary motivations for synthetic data generation and three major categories of future applications were highlighted. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The content is the same (five motivations, three application categories); the publication increased stated certainty but did not change substantive content.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Future work directions include exploring new domains and less commonly used data types, as well as developing evaluation benchmarks and standard generative models. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Future work should focus on developing evaluation benchmarks and standardized generative models tailored to specific healthcare domains. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes and clarifies the recommendation to prioritize benchmarks and standardized models tailored to domains, making the future-work recommendation more focused.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Metabolic syndrome (MetS) is associated with an increased incidence of non-communicable diseases (NCDs) such as type II diabetes mellitus, cardiovascular diseases, and cancer. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Metabolic syndrome (MetS) is associated with an increased incidence of non-communicable diseases (NCDs) such as type II diabetes mellitus, cardiovascular diseases, and cancer. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Identical high-level conclusion in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Evaluated machine learning (ML) techniques, with accuracy ranging from 75.5% to 98.9%, can more accurately diagnose MetS than both statistical and risk quantification techniques. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Evaluated machine learning (ML) techniques, with accuracy ranging from 75.5% to 98.9%, can more accurately diagnose MetS than both statistical and risk quantification techniques. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The result about ML techniques outperforming others and reported accuracy range is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The decision tree and artificial neural network ML techniques have the highest predictive performance for the prediction of MetS. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The decision tree and artificial neural network ML techniques have the highest predictive performance for the prediction of MetS. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The substantive claim about which techniques perform best is the same, but the publication downgrades the stated certainty from high to medium.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The standardized Z-score is the most frequent statistical technique identified for the diagnosis of MetS. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The standardized Z-score is the most frequent statistical technique identified for the diagnosis of MetS. (certainty=medium, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: AI-generated reports prioritized patient concerns and streamlined documentation for providers. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: EMBRACE achieved 93% recall, 91% precision, and 0.91 R² error in predicting 5-class activities, 4-class future burnout levels, and 1 clinically explainable survey on the collected dataset. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication gives concrete performance metrics on the collected dataset (presumably the reported real-time data), providing stronger evidence than the preprint's descriptive evaluation.

alignments.pub_claim_text: On the WESAD dataset, the model achieved 94.1% recall and 94.6% precision for 3-class stress level prediction. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: Validation of proprietary and publicly available datasets demonstrates the robustness and generalizability of the EMBRACE model. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)

alignments.pub_claim_text: The NLP validation of the top 200 retrieved patients showed high performance for suicidal ideation with an AUROC of 98.6 and for suicide attempt with an AUROC of 97.3. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: Combining NLP and diagnostic codes, while accounting for negated suicide expressions in notes, produced the best performance in case extraction. (certainty=medium, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: Two downregulated genes, ATP5L and ATP5H, which encode subunits of ATP synthase, may play a role in Alzheimer's disease. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed model achieved an AUROC score of 0.879, indicating strong predictive performance. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed model achieved an AUROC score of 0.879, indicating strong predictive performance. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: Longer mechanical ventilation times are associated with higher mortality rates among patients. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Subgroup analysis indicated that models with lab-only predictors outperformed those with mixed or no-lab predictors. (certainty=medium, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: This method achieves standardized and accurate virtual peritoneal lavage cytology and holds great potential for clinical translation. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)

alignments.pub_claim_text: The system achieves 87.50% accuracy for tertiary classification of Normal, COVID-19, and Pneumonia cases using transfer learning.
alignments.rationale: The publication carries the same tertiary classification accuracy claim verbatim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The CNN trained from scratch achieves 93.75% accuracy for tertiary classification.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The CNN trained from scratch achieves 93.75% accuracy for tertiary classification.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same result for the CNN trained from scratch.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Classification accuracy drops with an increased number of classes in the case of transfer learning.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The classification accuracy decreases with the increased number of classes in the transfer learning approach.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same observation about reduced accuracy with more classes in transfer learning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Contrast-enhanced mammography (CEM) demonstrates improved diagnostic performance compared to standard 2D mammography.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report or reiterate a direct comparison between CEM and standard 2D mammography; that preprint claim is not present.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Deep learning (DL) can improve CEM diagnostic precision.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Deep learning (DL) can improve contrast-enhanced mammography (CEM) diagnostic precision.
alignments.rationale: The publication affirms the same DL-improvement claim but with stronger presentation/certainty and elevated emphasis, effectively amplifying confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There is a relatively small number of studies evaluating different DL algorithms for CEM.
alignments.pub_claim_text: There is a relatively small number of studies evaluating different DL algorithms, and most studies are retrospective.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the limited-number observation but reports the overall limitation with slightly less certainty in tone.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Most studies evaluating DL applications for CEM are retrospective.
alignments.pub_claim_text: There is a relatively small number of studies evaluating different DL algorithms, and most studies are retrospective.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the retrospective-study limitation but frames it within a combined limitation statement and somewhat lower stated certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Further prospective testing to assess the performance of DL applications in actual clinical settings is warranted.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not explicitly include the recommendation for further prospective testing as stated in the preprint.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Models incorporating attention mechanisms achieved accuracies of 88.1% and 89.1%.
alignments.rationale: The publication adds specific model performance results (attention-based models) that were not present in the preprint.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Prospective studies reported AUC values of 0.89 and 0.91.
alignments.rationale: The publication includes prospective-study AUC results not present in the preprint.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Combining DL models with radiomics features improved classification.
alignments.rationale: The publication introduces the finding that combining DL with radiomics improves classification; this specific result was absent from the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed classification model can analyze chest X-rays to help in the accurate diagnosis of COVID-19.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed classification model can analyze chest X-rays and assist in the accurate diagnosis of COVID-19.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the preprint's contribution claim about the proposed model assisting COVID-19 diagnosis from chest X-rays.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The deep learning model VGG16 achieved an accuracy of 95.9% for classifying pneumonia, tuberculosis, and normal cases.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The deep learning model VGG-16 achieved a test accuracy of 95.9% for classifying pneumonia, tuberculosis, and normal X-rays.
alignments.rationale: The publication reproduces the same VGG-16 test accuracy result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The DenseNet-161 model achieved an accuracy of 98.9% for the segregation of normal pneumonia and COVID-19.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The DenseNet-161 model achieved a test accuracy of 98.9% for the segregation of normal pneumonia and COVID-19.
alignments.rationale: The publication presents the same DenseNet-161 accuracy result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: ResNet-18 achieved an accuracy of up to 76% for severity classification of COVID-19.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The ResNet-18 model achieved a test accuracy of up to 76% for severity classification of COVID-19.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same ResNet-18 accuracy for severity classification.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The approach allows for mass screening of people using X-rays as a primary validation for COVID-19.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not make the explicit claim about enabling mass screening with X-rays as primary validation; that preprint claim is omitted.
