alignments.pub_claim_text: Artificial intelligence techniques can be used to identify the presence and severity of COVID-19 infection from chest X-ray images.
alignments.rationale: The publication includes this broader, higher-level claim (identifying presence and severity) that was not explicitly stated in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The deep learning model generates dementia scores for the Clock Drawing Test images with an Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC) of 81.3% ± 4.3%.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The deep learning model predicted dementia status with an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC) of 81.3% ± 4.3% when only the CDT images were used.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same CDT-only AUC and interpretation as the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A composite logistic regression model using age and the generated dementia scores yielded an average AUC of 92% ± 0.8% and an average weighted F1 score of 94.4% ± 0.7%.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A composite logistic regression model using age, level of education, and the predictions from the CDT-only model yielded an average AUC of 91.9% ±1.1% and an average F1 score of 94.6% ±0.4%.
alignments.rationale: The publication modifies the composite model (adds education as a covariate) and reports slightly different performance metrics, changing the specific claim about the composite model.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model offers a cost-effective and easily deployable mechanism for detecting cognitive impairment online, without the need to visit a clinic.
alignments.pub_claim_text: This approach can offer a cost-effective and easily deployable mechanism for detecting cognitive impairment when fully validated.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the deployment/low-cost claim but adds the important caveat that full validation is required, tempering the original unqualified deployment claim.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The modeling framework establishes a proof-of-principle that deep learning can be applied on images derived from the CDT to predict dementia status.
alignments.rationale: The publication explicitly frames the work as a proof-of-principle statement, which was not separately stated as a distinct claim in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Deep learning models can significantly improve the detection of complex congenital heart disease (CHD) in fetal surveys.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Applied to guideline-recommended imaging, ensemble learning models could significantly improve detection of fetal CHD.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the improvement claim but adds the restriction to guideline-recommended imaging and specifies ensemble models, narrowing and clarifying the original claim and reducing stated certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model achieved an AUC of 0.99, 95% sensitivity, 96% specificity, and 100% negative predictive value in distinguishing normal from abnormal hearts.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model achieved an area under the curve (AUC) of 0.99 with 95% sensitivity and 96% specificity in distinguishing normal from abnormal hearts.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same performance metrics (AUC, sensitivity, specificity) with equivalent meaning; the NPV omission is a minor reporting difference but does not change the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Sensitivity of the deep learning model was comparable to that of clinicians and remained robust on external and lower-quality images.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Model sensitivity was comparable to that of clinicians and remained robust on outside-hospital and lower-quality images.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the comparability and robustness claim; its certainty label is stronger (high) so confidence is amplified relative to the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Cardiac measurements obtained from the model correlated with reported measures for normal and abnormal hearts.
alignments.rationale: No corresponding claim about cardiac measurement correlation appears in the publication, so this result appears omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This study represents the first use of deep learning to approximately double standard clinical performance in detecting fetal CHD.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not assert a 'first use' or an explicit doubling of clinical performance, so this strong contribution claim was removed.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: GEM ranked over 90% of causal genes among the top or second candidate using manually curated or CNLP derived phenotypes.
alignments.pub_claim_text: GEM ranked over 90% of the causal genes among the top or second candidate and prioritized for review a median of 3 candidate genes per case.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the >90% ranking claim and adds detail about median number of candidates prioritized, clarifying but not changing the core result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: In 17 of 20 cases with diagnostic structural variants, GEM identified the causal structural variants as the top or second candidate.
alignments.pub_claim_text: In 17 of 20 cases with diagnostic structural variants (SVs), GEM identified the causal SVs as the top candidate.
alignments.rationale: The publication tightens the result from 'top or second' to 'top candidate', which is a revision and narrows the claim's scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: GEM enables automated diagnostic interpretation of whole exome sequencing and whole genome sequencing for all types of variants, including structural variants.
alignments.pub_claim_text: GEM enabled diagnostic interpretation inclusive of all variant types through automated nomination of a very short list of candidate genes and disorders for final review and reporting.
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same inclusive automated interpretation claim but adds the operational detail of nominating a very short list, clarifying how the system is used.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The analysis of 14 previously unsolved cases demonstrated the utility of GEM for reanalysis, providing novel findings in one case.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Analysis of 14 previously unsolved cases resulted in a novel finding for one case.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same outcome from the reanalysis (one novel finding), matching the preprint's result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: GEM has the potential to decrease the cost and speed up the case review process in genetic disease diagnosis.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not explicitly claim cost reduction or faster case review, so this prospective operational benefit was not retained.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning models have a better predictive accuracy in detecting withdrawal from legal proceedings in intimate partner violence cases compared to statistical techniques.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning models provide a more accurate way of predicting the decision of intimate partner violence victims to withdraw from prosecution compared to previous statistical models.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same comparative accuracy claim with equivalent meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The addition of one new variable to the previous study's subset improves the accuracy of detecting withdrawal by 7.5%.
alignments.pub_claim_text: By adding one new variable to the predictive model from previous studies, the accuracy of detecting withdrawal improved by 7.5%.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same quantified improvement from adding one variable, matching the preprint result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This study presents a novel application of machine learning techniques to predict the decision of victims of intimate partner violence to withdraw from prosecution.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The application of explainable artificial intelligence techniques allowed for the identification of the most informative input features in predicting withdrawal from legal proceedings.
alignments.rationale: The publication focuses on explainable-AI and feature identification, which clarifies the methodological novelty claimed in the preprint by specifying the techniques and their outcome.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Deep learning models predicted ASD with the highest diagnostic accuracy and generalized well to other MRI datasets.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Deep learning models predicted ASD with the highest diagnostic accuracy and generalized well to other MRI datasets.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same high-level result without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our model achieves state-of-the-art 80% area under the ROC curve (AUROC) in diagnosis on test data from the IMPAC dataset.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our model achieves state-of-the-art 80% area under the ROC curve (AUROC) in diagnosis on test data from the IMPAC dataset.
alignments.rationale: Identical performance claim in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The highest performing models identified reproducible putative biomarkers for accurate ASD diagnosis in accord with known ASD markers as well as novel cerebellum biomarkers.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The highest performing models identified reproducible putative biomarkers for accurate ASD diagnosis in accord with known ASD markers as well as novel cerebellar biomarkers.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same biomarker identification claim (minor wording change only).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Such reproducibility lends credence to their tremendous potential for defining and using a set of truly generalizable ASD biomarkers.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The reproducibility of identified biomarkers lends credence to their tremendous potential for defining and using a set of truly generalizable ASD biomarkers.
alignments.rationale: The concluding claim about potential generalizable biomarkers is retained verbatim in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The AITongue model could screen PLGC individuals with an AUC of 0.75, which is 10.3% higher than that of the model constructed with gastric cancer-related canonical risk factors.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The AITongue model could screen PLGC individuals with an AUC of 0.75, which is 10.3% higher than the model that only included canonical risk factors.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same performance comparison and AUC figure with equivalent meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The AITongue model reached an AUC of 0.71 in predicting PLGC risk when validated in a prospective PLGC follow-up cohort.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The AITongue model reached an AUC of 0.71 in predicting PLGC risk in a prospective follow-up cohort.
alignments.rationale: The prospective validation AUC claim is reported identically in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study has demonstrated the value of tongue image characteristics in PLGC screening and risk prediction.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The AITongue model uncovered potential associations between tongue image characteristics and precancerous lesions of gastric cancer (PLGC).
alignments.rationale: The preprint's stronger wording ('demonstrated the value') is softened in the publication to 'uncovered potential associations', indicating a revision and reduced confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A smartphone-based App screening system has been developed to enhance the application convenience of the AITongue model in the natural population.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A smartphone-based app screening system was developed to enhance the application convenience of the AITongue model in high-risk areas of gastric cancer in China.
alignments.rationale: The publication specifies the deployment context (high-risk areas in China) versus the preprint's broader 'natural population', narrowing and clarifying the contribution.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our model achieves a high (89.4%) ROC AUC in identifying known disease subtypes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our model achieves a high (89.4%) ROC AUC in identifying known disease subtypes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The preprint and publication text are identical in wording and claim content, so no change is detected.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We identify 515 disease candidates predicted to possess previously unannotated subtypes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We identify 515 disease candidates predicted to possess previously unannotated subtypes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The number and framing of the candidate list are unchanged between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our models can partition diseases into distinct subtypes. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our models can partition diseases into distinct subtypes. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Conclusion wording and certainty are preserved exactly in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This methodology enables a robust, scalable approach for improving knowledge-based annotations. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This methodology enables a robust, scalable approach for improving knowledge-based annotations. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The methodological claim and confidence level are unchanged in the published version.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our candidates are attractive targets for further study and personalized medicine. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our candidates are attractive targets for further study and personalized medicine. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The suggestion for downstream study and translational relevance is unchanged between versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Artificial intelligence models applied to 12-lead electrocardiogram waveforms can predict atrial fibrillation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The prediction from the ECG-AI model was estimated to have a higher correlation with AF than that from the clinical variable model. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the general predictive claim into a specific comparative performance result (correlation vs clinical model) and reports a lower certainty, so the claim is clarified and somewhat narrowed/tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We identified three signals at established atrial fibrillation susceptibility loci marked by the sarcomeric gene TTN, and sodium channel genes SCN5A and SCN10A. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: In the ECG-AI GWAS, we identified 3 signals at established AF susceptibility loci marked by the sarcomeric gene TTN and sodium channel genes SCN5A and SCN10A. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The genomic loci and phrasing are the same in the publication, indicating no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Predicted atrial fibrillation risk from an ECG-AI model is influenced by genetic variation implicating sarcomeric, ion channel, and height pathways. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Predicted AF risk from an ECG-AI model is influenced by genetic variation implicating sarcomeric, ion channel and body height pathways. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The pathway-level conclusion is preserved but reported with higher certainty in the publication, so the semantic content is unchanged and confidence is amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: ECG-AI models may identify individuals at risk for disease via specific biological pathways. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: ECG-AI models may identify individuals at risk for disease via specific biological pathways. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The general conclusion about pathway-based risk identification is restated without substantive change.

alignments.pub_claim_text: We also identified 2 novel loci near the genes VGLL2 and EXT1. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This genomic finding (two novel loci) appears in the publication but was not present in the preprint, so it is an added claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The first principal component (PC1) exhibits a greater alignment with the Y axis, followed by the Z axis, with a minimal correlation observed with the X axis. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The specific axis-alignment detail for PC1 is reported in the preprint but does not appear in the publication's claim list, so it was removed.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The pairs of Breast and Lung cancer, as well as Breast and Renal cancer, exhibit the most notable distinctions in their anatomical spreading patterns. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Notable differences in anatomical spreading patterns were identified between Breast and Lung cancer, as well as Breast and Renal cancer. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The same pairwise differences are reported verbatim in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The SVM algorithm achieved a 97% accuracy rate when using a polynomial kernel and TabNet achieved a 96% accuracy in distinguishing the distribution of brain metastases for different primary cancers. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The SVM algorithm achieved 97% accuracy in distinguishing brain metastases distribution for different primary cancers. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The SVM accuracy result is preserved; the publication statement omits the TabNet figure in the short claim list but the SVM result matches exactly.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The RF algorithm ranks PC1 as the most important discriminating feature. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The RF algorithm ranked PC1 as the most important discriminating feature in the analysis of brain metastases. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The RF/PC1 importance claim is carried over without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The results demonstrate an accurate multiclass machine learning classification with respect to the distribution of brain metastases. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Anatomical brain metastases distributions differ based on primary cancer type. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The preprint's general conclusion about accurate multiclass classification is reframed in the publication as the underlying finding (distribution differences); the publication emphasizes the biological finding rather than the broader classifier claim, so the claim is clarified and somewhat narrowed.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: An AI chatbot significantly outperformed manual data extraction in accuracy for ultrasound and operative reports. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: AI chatbot significantly outperformed manual data extraction in accuracy for ultrasound and operative reports. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The primary performance claim is stated identically in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The AI chatbot correctly assigned the Pediatric Appendicitis Grade (PAG) score with fewer errors than manual extraction. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The appendicitis grade was erroneously assigned manually in 29 patients (28.2%), and by ChatGPT-4 in 3 (2.9%). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication provides numeric error counts that correspond directly to the preprint's statement about fewer chatbot errors.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The use of the AI chatbot was able to avoid misclassification in 59.2% of the records and was approximately 100 times faster than manual extraction. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The use of the AI chatbot was able to avoid misclassification in 59.2% of the records including both reports and extracted data approximately 40 times faster. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The misclassification avoidance percentage is unchanged but the reported speedup was revised downward (from ~100x to ~40x) and the scope of the 59.2% is clarified, so the claim is revised and confidence in the speed magnitude is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Wider validation is required and data safety concerns must be fully addressed for the novel AI tools. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Wider validation is required and data safety concerns must be addressed. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The limitation and its emphasis on validation and safety are preserved, with only minor wording differences that do not change the substantive meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: GPT-4o achieved 80% agreement in extracting research objectives and background and 100% in extracting research design. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: GPT-4o achieved 80% agreement in extracting research objectives and background and 100% in extracting research design. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The performance figures and phrasing are identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: GPTs with customized prompts produced more accurate and consistent outputs than standard prompts. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: GPTs with customized prompts produced more accurate and consistent outputs than standard prompts. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The claim about customized prompts is restated verbatim in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This study suggests the potential utility of generative AI in pre-institutional review board (IRB) review tasks. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This study suggests the potential utility of generative AI in pre-institutional review board (IRB) review tasks. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion about potential utility for pre-IRB tasks is unchanged between versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Limitations include the absence of gold standard reference data, reliance on a single evaluator, and lack of convergence and inter-rater reliability analysis. (certainty=high, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Limitations include the absence of gold standard reference data and reliance on a single evaluator. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The publication shortens the listed limitations (dropping convergence/inter-rater reliability) and reduces stated certainty, so the limitation set is revised and confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This study offers important early insights into how generative AI could assist the ethical evaluation of clinical research documents written in Japanese. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This study suggests the potential utility of generative AI in pre-institutional review board (IRB) review tasks. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the general utility claim but removes the language-specific framing (Japanese) and expresses lower certainty, so the claim is clarified and narrowed in scope with tempered confidence.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Both GPT-4 and GPT-4o demonstrated high reproducibility across ten trials. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The reproducibility result appears in the publication but was not present in the preprint, indicating a new (added) claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Deep Learning techniques have shown an enormous breakthrough in the field of medical diagnostics.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Deep learning-based algorithms achieve the best results for pneumonia classification with an accuracy of 98.7%, a sensitivity of 0.99, and a specificity of 0.98.
alignments.rationale: The publication gives a much more specific, quantitative claim about DL performance for pneumonia classification, narrowing the original broad claim about medical diagnostics and increasing the expressed confidence with exact metrics.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Various automated systems have been proposed for the rapid detection of pneumonia on chest x-ray images.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Deep learning-based algorithms achieve the best results for pneumonia classification with an accuracy of 98.7%, a sensitivity of 0.99, and a specificity of 0.98.
alignments.rationale: Both refer to automated pneumonia-detection on chest X‑rays; the publication changes a general statement about proposed systems into a concrete claim about top-performing DL algorithms with high performance metrics.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The review analyzes the usability, goodness factors, and computational complexities of the algorithms that implement these techniques.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A detailed review summarizing the literature and offering guidelines for medical practitioners on pneumonia detection is lacking.
alignments.rationale: The preprint asserts that such a review analyzes these factors, whereas the publication asserts that a detailed review/guideline is lacking; this reverses the earlier claim and reduces its certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The paper discusses the quality, usability, and size of the available datasets, and ways of coping with unbalanced datasets.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The majority of the applied datasets for pneumonia detection are highly unbalanced and limited, providing unreliable results and rendering methods unsuitable for large-scale use.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the dataset-focused concern from the preprint but makes it stronger and more specific, emphasizing severe imbalance/limitations and implications for large-scale applicability.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study examined the lexical stability of clinical notes over a period of more than ten years.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The lexical stability of clinical notes from psychiatric services was assessed over a period of more than ten years.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same assessment of lexical stability over the same time period.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Lexical and syntactic stability was found in clinical notes from the Psychiatric Services of the Central Denmark Region.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Lexical stability was found in the clinical notes, with only minor deviations during the COVID-19 pandemic.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the stability finding but adds nuance about minor deviations during the COVID-19 period.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The stability of clinical notes over time supports the use of Natural Language Processing for predictive modeling in clinical practice.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The findings suggest that the stability of clinical notes bodes well for the use of NLP in predictive modeling in clinical psychiatry.
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same conclusion that stability supports the use of NLP for predictive modeling in clinical psychiatry.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our deep learning model predicted the need for ventilation with a high accuracy, sensitivity and specificity (90.06%, 86.34% and 84.38%, respectively).
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our deep learning model predicted the need for mechanical ventilation with a high accuracy, sensitivity and specificity (90.06%, 86.34% and 84.38%, respectively).
alignments.rationale: Identical result claim and metrics are reported in both preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The prediction was done approximately three days ahead of the actual intubation event.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The prediction of the need for mechanical ventilation was done approximately 3 days ahead of the actual intubation event.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same temporal prediction claim with the same value.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our model outperformed two Pulmonary and Critical Care experts who evaluated the same X-ray images and provided an incremental accuracy of 7.24–13.25%.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our model outperformed two Pulmonary and Critical Care experts who evaluated the same X-ray images, providing an incremental accuracy of 7.24%-13.25%.
alignments.rationale: The comparative performance claim versus experts is identical in preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our deep learning model accurately predicted the need for mechanical ventilation early during hospitalization of COVID-19 patients.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our deep learning model accurately predicted the need for mechanical ventilation early during hospitalisation of patients with COVID-19.
alignments.rationale: The conclusion that the model accurately predicted early need for ventilation in COVID-19 patients is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Prognostic stratification as provided by our model is likely to be highly valuable until effective preventive or treatment measures become widely available for COVID-19 patients.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Prognostic stratification as provided by our model is likely to be highly valuable until effective preventive or treatment measures become widely available for patients with COVID-19.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the same conclusion about the likely value of prognostic stratification until treatments are available.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Most automatic hippocampal segmentation methods, especially deep learning-based ones, performed well on public datasets but showed more error and variability on unseen data.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Most automatic hippocampal segmentation methods, particularly deep learning-based ones trained on manual labels, performed well on public datasets.
alignments.rationale: The publication keeps the finding of good performance on public datasets but omits the preprint's explicit statement about higher error/variability on unseen data, narrowing the claim's scope and tempering generalizability concerns.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Many segmentation methods tended to over-segment, particularly at the anterior hippocampus border, but were able to distinguish between healthy controls, MCI, and dementia patients based on hippocampal volume.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Many segmentation methods tended to over-segment, especially at the anterior hippocampus border.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the over-segmentation observation but separates/omits the additional component about diagnostic discrimination present in the preprint, narrowing the focus to the segmentation error.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: More publicly accessible datasets with manual labels are required for automatic hippocampal segmentations to be accurate and reliable, particularly for clinical populations.
alignments.pub_claim_text: There is a need for more publicly accessible datasets with manual labels across diverse ages and pathological conditions.
alignments.rationale: Both assert the need for more labeled public datasets; the publication broadens the recommendation to emphasize diversity of ages and pathologies beyond the preprint's clinical-population focus.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The lack of variance within training sets currently prevents the transfer of learning to novel data acquired clinically.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not explicitly state that lack of variance prevents transfer to clinical data; this specific causal limitation is not present in the published claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed deep neural network (DNN) framework can efficiently detect predictive features in genomic data.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed deep neural network (DNN) based prediction modeling framework can efficiently detect predictive features.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same result that the DNN framework efficiently detects predictive features.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The DNN framework accurately predicts disease risk compared to many existing methods.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The DNN framework accurately predicts disease risk compared to many existing methods.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the comparative predictive-performance claim intact.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The method is biologically interpretable as it is built based on selected predictive genes.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The DNN framework is biologically interpretable as it is built based on the selected predictive genes.
alignments.rationale: The interpretability claim is repeated verbatim in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed method can be applied to whole-genome sequencing data that have millions of potential predictors.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed method is computationally efficient and can be applied to genome-wide data.
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes computational efficiency and explicitly states applicability to genome-wide data, clarifying and supporting the preprint's claim about whole-genome application.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The DNN framework addresses the curse of dimensionality and improves biological interpretability in deep learning applications.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed deep neural network (DNN) based prediction modeling framework can efficiently detect predictive features.
alignments.rationale: The publication's claim about efficient detection of predictive features aligns with the preprint's claim about addressing high dimensionality and improving interpretability, though the publication frames it more as an empirical capability than an explicit methodological claim about the curse of dimensionality.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: COVID-19 causes serious fatalities with the United States recording over 3,000 deaths within 24 hours, the highest in the world for a single day.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not include this pandemic-fatality statistic or any equivalent single-day death-count claim; it appears to have been omitted from the final paper.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed framework combines machine learning models and IoT technology to combat COVID-19 in smart cities.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study presents a novel framework that combines machine learning models and IoT technology to combat COVID-19 in smart cities.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same contribution claim with nearly identical wording and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning algorithms can detect, prevent, and trace the spread of COVID-19, providing a better understanding of the virus in smart cities.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning algorithms can detect, prevent, and trace the spread of COVID-19, providing a better understanding of the disease in smart cities.
alignments.rationale: The result claim is preserved verbatim aside from changing 'virus' to 'disease', keeping meaning and certainty the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The machine learning framework presented in this study has the potential to help national healthcare systems in curtailing the COVID-19 pandemic in smart cities.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed machine learning framework has the potential to help national healthcare systems in curtailing the COVID-19 pandemic in smart cities.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains this conclusion nearly verbatim and with the same level of qualification about potential impact.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed framework is poised to generate research interests that will yield outcomes capable of forming an improved framework.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed framework is intended to generate research interests that could lead to an improved framework for combating COVID-19.
alignments.rationale: The publication expresses the same forward-looking intent but uses slightly clearer, more conservative phrasing ('intended' / 'could lead'), so the claim is clarified rather than substantially changed.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We computed the best-imbalanced machine learning models to predict disease worsening and the removal of the biosimilar, with f1-scores of 0.52 and 0.63, respectively.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The best-imbalanced machine learning models to predict disease worsening and the removal of the biosimilar achieved f1-scores of 0.52 and 0.63, respectively.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the numeric model performance claim with essentially identical wording and metrics.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Biosimilar drugs do not always work well for rheumatic diseases.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Biosimilar drugs do not always work well for rheumatic diseases.
alignments.rationale: The conclusion about variable biosimilar effectiveness is preserved verbatim in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: After switching from the reference drug to a biosimilar drug, patients have a high withdrawal rate due to ineffectiveness.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not explicitly state a high withdrawal rate due to ineffectiveness after switching; that specific prevalence/result is absent from the published claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our machine learning model (imbalanced machine learning) is able to predict when drug withdrawal and disease activity occur.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The best-imbalanced machine learning models to predict disease worsening and the removal of the biosimilar achieved f1-scores of 0.52 and 0.63, respectively.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same predictive capability but presents it as quantified model performance (f1-scores); this clarifies the claim by adding performance metrics.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: In our decision trees appear not previously studied variables, such as age, switching, or optimization.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The decision tree models identified important factors such as switching of biosimilar and age for predicting disease worsening.
alignments.rationale: The publication keeps the core claim that decision trees identified age and switching as important factors but frames it more directly as identified predictors; optimization is handled separately in another published claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: ChatGPT demonstrated a triage accuracy of 87.2% for ophthalmological emergency case vignettes.
alignments.pub_claim_text: ChatGPT demonstrated a triage accuracy of 93.6% for ophthalmological emergency case vignettes.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same triage outcome but with a different (higher) accuracy percentage, representing a quantitative revision that increases reported performance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Most answers provided by ChatGPT contained only appropriate recommendations for preclinical measures.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Most answers provided by ChatGPT contained only appropriate recommendations for prehospital measures.
alignments.rationale: The publication corrects/clarifies the setting terminology from 'preclinical' to the more standard 'prehospital', preserving the substantive claim about appropriateness of recommendations.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There was an overall potential to inflict harm to users/patients in 32% of the answers given by ChatGPT.
alignments.pub_claim_text: There was an overall potential to inflict harm to users/patients in 32% of the answers given by ChatGPT.
alignments.rationale: The risk-of-harm percentage and claim are carried over identically into the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: ChatGPT should not be used as a stand-alone primary source of information about acute ophthalmological symptoms.
alignments.pub_claim_text: ChatGPT should presently not be used as a stand-alone primary source of information about acute ophthalmological symptoms.
alignments.rationale: The publication adds 'presently', softening timing slightly and clarifying current applicability while preserving the core recommendation.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The safety and efficacy of AI in the preclinical management of ophthalmological emergencies must be reassessed regularly as AI evolves.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The safety and efficacy of AI in the prehospital management of ophthalmological emergencies must be reassessed regularly as AI evolves.
alignments.rationale: The publication amends the term 'preclinical' to 'prehospital', clarifying the clinical context without changing the substantive recommendation.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Approximately 20% of children receiving silver diamine fluoride fail to respond to treatment.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Approximately 20% of children receiving silver diamine fluoride fail to respond to treatment.
alignments.rationale: The result is reported identically in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Bivariate group comparisons of bacterial abundance indicate a number of genera were significantly different between nonresponders and those who responded to SDF therapy.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Bivariate group comparisons of bacterial abundance indicate a number of genera were significantly different between non-responders and those who responded to SDF therapy.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the bivariate comparison result with equivalent wording and meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Prevotella pallens and Veillonella denticariosi were retained in full lasso models and combined with clinical variables in a six-input multilayer perceptron.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Prevotella pallens and Veillonella denticariosi were retained in full lasso models and combined with clinical variables in a six-input multilayer perceptron.
alignments.rationale: The modeling result is carried forward unchanged into the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The acidogenic and acid-tolerant nature of retained bacterial species may overcome the antimicrobial effects of silver diamine fluoride.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The acidogenic and acid-tolerant nature of retained bacterial species may overcome the antimicrobial effects of silver diamine fluoride.
alignments.rationale: The mechanistic conclusion appears identically in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Further research to validate the model in larger external samples is needed.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Further research to validate the model in larger external samples is needed.
alignments.rationale: The limitation/request for external validation remains, but the publication notes a lower certainty level (limitation rated 'low'), so the confidence attached to this claim is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The AI model demonstrated statistically significant differences in Dice scores between white subjects and minority ethnic groups, indicating racial bias.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The AI model demonstrated statistically significant differences in Dice scores between races, with white individuals scoring approximately 94% and minority ethnic groups scoring between 86-89%.
alignments.rationale: The publication provides numerical detail and explicit score ranges, clarifying and quantifying the preprint's reported Dice-score differences without changing the substantive finding.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The AI model showed biased performance against minority racial groups in volumetric and functional measures, even after correcting for possible confounders.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The AI model was biased against minority racial groups in volumetric and functional measures, even after correction for possible confounders.
alignments.rationale: The result about biased performance after confounder correction is preserved verbatim and unchanged in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study concluded that racial bias exists in deep learning-based cine CMR segmentation models due to the imbalanced nature of the training data.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Racial bias can exist in deep learning-based cine CMR segmentation models when trained with a database that is sex-balanced but not race-balanced.
alignments.rationale: The publication refines the causal explanation by specifying the training-data imbalance context (sex-balanced but not race-balanced), clarifying the preprint's broader causal statement.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The results indicated that there was no sex bias in the AI model's performance, despite the UK Biobank database being sex-balanced.
alignments.pub_claim_text: No covariate could explain the Dice score bias between racial groups according to the multivariate linear regression analysis.
alignments.rationale: The publication's multivariate analysis conclusion implies that sex (a covariate) did not explain the racial Dice-score bias, which matches the preprint's statement that there was no sex bias.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Word embeddings most similar to 'Ceiling of Treatment' clustered around phrases describing end-of-life care and resuscitation discussions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Word embeddings with most similarity to 'Ceiling of Treatment' clustered around phrases describing end-of-life care, ceiling of care and LST discussions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same result with slightly expanded terminology (adds 'ceiling of care' and 'LST'), so the claim is clarified but essentially unchanged in scope and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The phrases have differing prognostic profiles with the highest 7-day mortality in phrases most implicitly referring to end of life, such as 'terminal care' and 'unsurvivable'. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The phrases have differing prognostic profile with the highest 7-day mortality in the phrases most explicitly referring to end of life. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both claims report the same pattern in 7-day mortality but the publication changes wording from 'implicitly' to 'explicitly', a substantive wording revision while maintaining the result and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: NLP can quantify and analyze real-world end-of-life discussions around prognosis and appropriate life-sustaining treatment. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Vocabulary used at end-of-life discussions are diverse and has a range of associations to 7-day mortality. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The preprint makes a broad capability claim about NLP; the publication narrows this to observations about vocabulary diversity and associations to mortality and uses a lower certainty, so the claim is clarified and somewhat tempered/narrowed.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning models (XGBoost) show excellent performance at predicting hypoglycaemia (AUROC: 0.998) and hyperglycaemia (AUROC: 0.989). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning models (XGBoost) show excellent performance at predicting hypoglycemia with an area under the receiver operating curve (AUROC) of 0.998 and average precision of 0.953. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication splits the preprint's combined result into separate statements and adds average-precision metrics for hypoglycemia; mapping is partial (hypoglycemia) so confidence in a perfect match is moderate.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Maximising model performance for blood glucose risk prediction and management is crucial to reduce the burden of alarm-fatigue on CGM users. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Maximizing model performance for glucose risk prediction and management is crucial to reduce the burden of alarm fatigue on CGM users. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this conclusion verbatim (minor wording differences only), so the claim is unchanged in meaning and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning enables more precise and timely predictions in comparison to baseline models. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning enables more precise and timely predictions in comparison with baseline models. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same comparative conclusion with equivalent certainty; wording is effectively identical.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SHAP helps identify what about a CGM user’s blood glucose control has led to predictions of risk. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: SHAP helps identify what about a CGM user's glucose control has led to predictions of risk which can be used to reduce their long-term risk of complications. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the interpretability claim but extends it to potential clinical utility (reducing long-term complication risk), broadening the asserted impact and amplifying the claim's implication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The newly developed point-of-care test for early-stage Lyme disease achieved a sensitivity of 90.5% and specificity of 87.0%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The newly developed point-of-care test for early-stage Lyme disease achieved a sensitivity of 90.5% and specificity of 87.0%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this result identically, so there is no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The point-of-care test outperformed previous Lyme disease tests based on area-under-the-curve (AUC) metrics. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The point-of-care test outperformed previous Lyme disease tests in terms of area-under-the-curve (AUC), sensitivity, and specificity. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication expands the comparative claim from AUC only to include sensitivity and specificity as well, broadening the performance comparison.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: With batch-specific standardization and threshold tuning, the specificity of the test improved to 96.3%, with an AUC of 0.963 and sensitivity of 85.7%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: With batch-specific standardization and threshold tuning, the specificity of the test improved to 96.3%, with an AUC of 0.963 and sensitivity of 85.7%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This detailed methodological result is reproduced exactly in the publication with the same metrics and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Swaasa AI platform was developed and validated for screening and prioritizing COVID-19 patients based on cough sound and symptoms. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Swaasa AI platform uses cough sound signatures and symptoms to screen and prioritize COVID-19 patients. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the platform's purpose and validation focus with equivalent wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Swaasa achieved 75.54% accuracy in detecting the likely presence of COVID-19 with 95.45% sensitivity and 73.46% specificity. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model demonstrated a 75.54% accuracy rate in detecting the likely presence of COVID-19 during clinical validation. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication separates the reported metrics; the accuracy figure matches exactly while sensitivity/specificity are reported in a different claim, so this alignment is a clarified split of the combined preprint statement.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Positive Predictive Value of Swaasa in identifying true COVID-19 positive subjects was 70.73%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Positive Predictive Value of the model was 70.73% based on pilot testing of 183 presumptive COVID subjects. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the PPV and adds the sample context (183 subjects), clarifying the result without changing its substantive meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Current rapid screening methods for COVID-19 are costly and require technical expertise. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Swaasa could serve as a cost-effective and remote monitoring tool for preliminary screening of potential COVID-19 subjects. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The preprint frames a limitation of existing methods; the publication pivots to framing Swaasa as a potential cost-effective alternative, so the statement is reframed and expanded toward an application.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Survival prediction integrates patient-specific molecular information and clinical signatures to forecast the anticipated time of an event. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Survival prediction integrates patient-specific molecular information and clinical signatures to forecast the anticipated time of an event. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this definitional/result claim verbatim, with no change in meaning or certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The widespread adoption of artificial intelligence (AI) methods in crafting survival predictors has revolutionized the field. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The widespread adoption of artificial intelligence (AI) methods in crafting survival predictors has revolutionized the field. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion about AI's impact is identical in the publication, so it is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The paper examines 22 existing review studies and provides a concise overview of their scope and limitations. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The paper examines 23 existing review studies and provides a concise overview of their scope and limitations. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication updates the count of review studies from 22 to 23; this is a factual revision without a change in scope or certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The analysis focuses on 74 recent survival predictors across 44 diverse diseases and delves into the methods used in their development. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The analysis focuses on 90 recent survival predictors across 44 diverse diseases and delves into the methods used in their development. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication increases the number of predictors analyzed from 74 to 90, a substantive change that broadens the study's coverage while retaining the same focus and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There is a need to centralize existing survival predictors knowledge and insights to catalyze advancements in prediction models. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: It is crucial to bring existing survival predictors knowledge and insights into a centralized platform to catalyze advancements. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication strengthens the language (from 'need' to 'crucial') and frames centralization as a concrete platform-oriented action, amplifying the assertiveness while keeping the same scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: AI demonstrates excellent accuracy in predicting T-DXd eligibility with a pooled sensitivity of 0.97 and specificity of 0.82. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: AI demonstrates high accuracy in predicting T-DXd eligibility for HER2 IHC scores, with a pooled sensitivity of 0.97 and specificity of 0.82. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reproduces the same performance metrics and claim about predicting T-DXd eligibility, so the claim is unchanged semantically and in certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: AI performs better in classifying HER2 scores, particularly in scores 2+ and 3+. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: AI holds promising potential in accurately identifying HER2-low patients and excels in distinguishing 2+ and 3+ scores. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the emphasis on better classification of 2+ and 3+ scores and connects it to HER2-low identification, clarifying and slightly expanding the original phrasing.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Substantial heterogeneity in AI performance was observed, with better results from deep learning and patch-based analysis. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Meta-regression revealed that AI performance is better with deep learning and patch-based analysis. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same meta-regression finding about superior performance for deep learning and patch-based approaches, so the claim is preserved.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: AI holds promising potential in accurately identifying HER2-low patients. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: AI holds promising potential in accurately identifying HER2-low patients and excels in distinguishing 2+ and 3+ scores. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication reiterates the same conclusion about identifying HER2-low patients, adding the related detail about 2+/3+ discrimination but not changing the core claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Upcoming validation studies should focus on enhancing AI’s precision in the 0-1+ range and improving the reporting of clinical and pre-analytical data. (certainty=low, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report this specific recommendation about improving precision in the 0-1+ range or reporting standards, so the preprint's limitation/recommendation is not present in the publication.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The DESTINY-Breast04 trial has demonstrated survival benefits of trastuzumab-deruxtecan (T-DXd) in metastatic breast cancer patients with low HER2 expression. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This trial result appears in the publication but was not present in the preprint; it is new contextual clinical evidence added in the paper.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed multi-task recurrent neural network architecture effectively handles multiple clinical outcomes and captures the dynamic nature of patients' status over time. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model predicts antipsychotic treatment outcomes in first-episode psychosis patients using longitudinal patient data. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication emphasizes the applied predictive task in a specific patient population rather than detailing or reiterating the multi-task RNN architecture claim, narrowing the scope and toning the original architectural effectiveness claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The predictive performance of the model improved from AUC values of 0.62 to 0.67 when including 1-week follow-up patient status. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Incorporating clinical data from multiple time points improved the model's performance, achieving AUC values of 0.72-0.74 for week 10 outcomes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports improved AUCs for later timepoints and different numeric values; the general claim of improvement remains but the reported effect sizes and time horizon are revised.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Incorporating prediction uncertainties and stratifying model decisions based on confidence allowed for accuracies above 0.8 for approximately 50% of patients in five out of six clinical scenarios. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model achieved accuracies above 0.8 for approximately 50% of patients in five out of six clinical scenarios after incorporating prediction uncertainties. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reproduces this exact performance claim linking incorporation of uncertainty to stratified high-accuracy predictions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study provides compelling evidence that leveraging time series data can achieve more accurate treatment outcome predictions in psychiatry. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Leveraging time series data significantly enhances the accuracy of treatment outcome predictions in psychiatry. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Both texts assert that time series data improves predictive accuracy; the publication states this similarly, so the conclusion is preserved.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The inclusion of time series data in individualized prediction modeling is advocated as it holds substantial promise in improving prognostic accuracy. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Leveraging time series data significantly enhances the accuracy of treatment outcome predictions in psychiatry. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the claim more strongly ('significantly enhances') compared with the preprint's advocacy, slightly amplifying the expressed confidence in benefit.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The integration of prediction uncertainty into the model allows for more reliable clinical decision-making. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication makes an explicit claim about clinical decision-making reliability tied to uncertainty integration that was not stated in the preprint, so this is an added interpretive/conclusion statement.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The ML algorithm predicted mortality with an area under receiver operating characteristic curve (AUROC) of 0.80. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The ML algorithm predicted mortality in patients with schizophrenia with an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUROC) of 0.80. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same AUROC result for mortality prediction, so the result claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Use or prescription of medications like antidepressants was associated with lower risk of death in patients with schizophrenia. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Use or prescription of medications like antidepressants was associated with a lower risk of death in patients with schizophrenia. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The association between antidepressant use and lower mortality appears in both documents with the same direction and similar certainty, so the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Abuse of alcohol/drugs and a diagnosis of delirium were associated with higher risk of death in patients with schizophrenia. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Abuse of alcohol/drugs and a diagnosis of delirium were associated with a higher risk of death in patients with schizophrenia. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Both versions report the same associations with higher mortality risk, so this result is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The ML models highlight the role of co-morbidities in determining mortality in patients with severe mental illness. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The ML models highlight the role of co-morbidities in determining mortality in patients with schizophrenia. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion about co-morbidities' role in mortality is present and unchanged between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The approach combines clinical knowledge, health data, and statistical learning to make predictions interpretable to clinicians. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The application of class-contrastive analysis to electronic healthcare record data is a step towards interpretable AI in the management of patients with schizophrenia. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication specifies the interpretability method (class-contrastive analysis) and frames it as a step toward interpretability; this clarifies the original contribution while slightly tempering the certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed multi-phase classifier achieved a mean F1-score of 92.09% on the internal test set of 358 scans. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed classifier achieved a mean F1 score of 92.09% on the internal test set of 358 scans. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the identical internal test F1 result, so the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model maintained high accuracy with mean F1-scores of 76.79% and 86.94% on the external test sets CTPAC-CCRCC and LiTS, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model maintained high accuracy with mean F1 scores of 76.79% and 86.94% on the CTPAC-CCRCC and LiTS datasets, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: External test set F1 scores are reported identically in the publication, so the result is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed method significantly outperformed state-of-the-art 3D approaches while requiring less computation time for inference. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed method significantly outperformed state-of-the-art 3D approaches while requiring less computation time for inference. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the claim of outperforming 3D approaches with reduced inference time, so it is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study demonstrates the potential of a precise, fast multi-phase classifier based on a 2D deep learning approach combined with a random sampling method. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed approach shows better accuracy with significantly reduced latency compared to state-of-the-art classification methods. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the conclusion more strongly (higher certainty and emphasis on reduced latency), clarifying and amplifying the preprint's reported potential.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The xDECIDE system offers personalized treatment options for cancer patients based on a combination of clinical evidence, expert insights, and real-world evidence. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: xDECIDE is a clinical decision support system that provides personalized treatment options for cancer patients. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Both texts assert that xDECIDE provides personalized treatment options based on integrated evidence; the publication restates this as a clinical decision support contribution.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Over 2,000 patients have enrolled in the XCELSIOR registry, with significant representation from various cancer types. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Over 7000 patients have enrolled in the XCELSIOR registry, with significant representation from various cancer types. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication updates the enrollment figure substantially (2,000 → 7,000), so the numeric result is revised and the scope (sample size) is broadened.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The xCORE AI-based treatment ranking algorithm has been deployed for validation in real-world patient populations. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The xCORE engine generates a ranked list of treatment options based on clinical evidence and expert insights. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication describes the engine's functionality but does not explicitly state the deployment-for-validation claim; thus the deployment assertion is toned down to a functional description, narrowing the operational claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The xDECIDE system is self-improving through machine learning and feedback mechanisms. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Over 500 treatment rationale statements have been encoded to enhance algorithm decision-making. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication provides a concrete mechanism (encoded rationale statements) that supports improved decision-making but does not explicitly claim ongoing self-improvement; this narrows and slightly tempers the original self-improving assertion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A Natural Language Processing method was used to uncover various issues and sentiments surrounding COVID-19 from social media. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A natural language processing method was used to uncover various issues and sentiments surrounding COVID-19 from social media. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The preprint and publication statements are effectively identical in content and certainty, so the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The BERT model was used for the sentiment classification of COVID-19 Reddit comments. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The BERT model was used for the sentiment classification of COVID-19 Reddit comments. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The wording and claim are the same in preprint and publication with unchanged certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: These findings shed light on the importance of studying trends and using computational techniques to assess human psyche in times of distress. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: These findings shed light on the importance of studying trends and using computational techniques to assess the human psyche in times of distress. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion is restated verbatim and retains the same scope and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Few-shot prompting of best-in-class LLMs are the best performing methods for extracting public stances towards vaccination. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Few-shot prompting of best-in-class LLMs are the best performing methods for extracting public stances towards vaccination. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The result is stated the same way in both versions with consistent certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: All alternatives to LLMs have significant risks of substantial misclassification. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: All alternatives to LLMs have significant risks of substantial misclassification. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this caution identically, so no semantic, scope, or confidence change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: LLMs offer a scalable tool for public health professionals to quickly gauge public opinion on health policies and interventions. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: LLMs have the potential to serve as a scalable tool for public health professionals to quickly gauge public opinion on health policies and interventions. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication softens the wording (adds "have the potential to serve"), which clarifies and slightly tempers the claim's assertiveness while keeping scope the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Incorporating LLMs into digital public health monitoring could greatly improve how we observe and react to dynamics in public health discussions. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The integration of LLMs into public health surveillance systems could substantially improve our ability to monitor and respond to changing public health attitudes. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same conclusion with minor wording changes ("monitor and respond" vs. "observe and react"), maintaining meaning and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The positive predictive value of the CNN was 95%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The convolutional neural network demonstrated a precision of 94% in diagnosing breast lesions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Precision/PPV correspond, but the reported value differs (95% vs 94%), so the numeric result was revised in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The best performance of 0.078264 was achieved at 36 epochs in the validation set. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: No matching training-epoch or metric claim appears in the publication claims, indicating this specific training-detail result was removed.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The grayscale intensity histogram of a USG image is an easy and feasible method to identify malignant lesions through an artificial neural network. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Simple neural networks can be effectively applied to diagnose malignant breast lesions in low-resource settings. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication drops the specific "grayscale histogram" detail and reframes the claim more broadly about simple neural networks (and with higher certainty), so the claim was revised and broadened with amplified confidence.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The trained convolutional neural network achieved an accuracy of 94.5% in classifying benign and malignant breast lesions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Accuracy for the trained CNN appears in the publication but was not present in the preprint claims, so this is an added quantitative result.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The sensitivity and specificity of the trained convolutional neural network were 94.9% and 94.1%, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Sensitivity and specificity metrics appear in the publication but were not claimed in the preprint, so these are added results.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The machine learning models tested failed to recognize 66% of the test cases involving injuries. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The models tested using synthesized cases fail to recognize 66% of the injuries for in-hospital mortality prediction. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both statements report the same failure rate and context (injuries in synthesized cases), so the result is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Most machine learning models tested inadequately responded to critically ill patients despite their overall performance metrics. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Most of the ML models tested fail to respond adequately to critically ill patients. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion is restated directly in the publication with the same meaning and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed testing framework acts as a critical bridge between machine learning model development and clinical deployment. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: No publication claim restates the specific contribution about the testing framework acting as a bridge, so this contribution appears to have been removed from the claim list.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Current machine learning models have major limitations and could be dangerous if used in hospitals without thorough testing. (certainty=high, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The models are unable to recognize severely impaired medical conditions or rapidly deteriorating health. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication states a specific limitation (failure to recognize severe/deteriorating conditions) that clarifies and narrows the preprint's broader safety warning about hospital use.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The study identifies similar deficiencies in the responsiveness of 5-year breast and lung cancer prediction models. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This result about deficiencies in 5-year models is new in the publication and has no counterpart in the preprint claims.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Incorporating medical knowledge into clinical machine learning models is an important future research direction. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds an explicit future-direction claim about incorporating medical knowledge that was not present in the preprint claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning classifiers, such as Random Forest, provided a significant improvement (∼29% in PR AUC and ∼5% in ROC AUC) compared to the Pneumonia Severity Index (PSI). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning classifiers, such as Random Forest, provided a statistically highly significant improvement (∼33% in PR AUC and ∼6% in ROC AUC) compared to PSI. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports the same comparative improvement but with different reported effect sizes (slightly larger), so the numeric claim was revised while retaining scope and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning classifiers required only 7 input values compared to the 20 parameters used in PSI. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning classifiers required only 7 input values compared to the 20 parameters used in PSI. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this factual claim exactly, so it is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There were statistically significant differences (p<0.05) between Random Forest and PSI among various races/ethnicities. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: No publication claim reports race/ethnicity subgroup differences, so this specific subgroup claim appears to have been removed.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: PSI remains a very strong clinical decision tool due to its ease of use, but machine learning classifiers can provide better prediction accuracy performance. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: PSI remains a very strong clinical decision tool due to its ease of use. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
