alignments.preprint_claim_text: Comparing prediction performance across multiple metrics such as PR AUC, instead of ROC AUC alone, can provide additional insight. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Comparing prediction performance across multiple metrics such as PR AUC can provide additional insight. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The recommendation is restated with equivalent meaning and certainty, so it is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We built a model with an accuracy of 100% in the correction of image orientation using EfficientNet. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A predictive model for the Sharp/van der Heijde score (SHS) was constructed using convolutional neural networks (CNN) and demonstrated 100% accuracy in image orientation correction. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both claims report 100% accuracy for image orientation correction; the publication frames the approach as CNN-based (broader term) rather than naming EfficientNet, so the core result is the same but slightly rephrased.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The EfficientNet-based model combined with the modules achieved correlation coefficients of 0.879 for erosion and 0.868 for joint space narrowing between the model and expert. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The κ values between the model and expert 1 for erosion and joint space narrowing were 0.879 and 0.865, respectively, indicating strong agreement. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our model replicated the known finding of erosion progression’s association with serologically positive patients. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: High-titre RF was identified as an independent risk factor for ΔSHS per year in biologics users, as predicted by the CNN model. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The preprint states a general replication of an association with seropositivity; the publication specifies high-titre RF and restricts the effect to biologics users and reports it with medium certainty, so the claim is more specific and presented with less certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our deep learning model showed a high performance in both joint space narrowing and erosion, demonstrating that deep learning models could be clinically applicable in estimating SHSs. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The κ values between the model and expert 1 for erosion and joint space narrowing were 0.879 and 0.865, respectively, indicating strong agreement. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication provides quantitative agreement metrics that support the preprint's assertion of high performance and potential clinical applicability; the publication phraseology is more result-focused than the broader clinical claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We demonstrated that a graph convolutional network (GCN) is a high performance model in predicting radiographic progression. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The AUCs of the GCN model for predicting ΔSHS >3/year were 0.753 and 0.734 for patients with and without biologics at baseline, respectively, which were superior to those of other models. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication gives GCN AUCs and states they outperform other models, directly supporting the preprint's claim of high GCN performance for predicting radiographic progression.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The strongest predictors of in-hospital mortality in acute STEMI patients include age, gender, cardiogenic shock, atrial fibrillation, ventricular fibrillation, third degree atrioventricular block, in-hospital bleeding, and history of hypertension, diabetes, and myocardial infarction. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The strongest predictors of in-hospital mortality in acute STEMI patients include age, gender, cardiogenic shock, atrial fibrillation, ventricular fibrillation, third degree atrioventricular block, in-hospital bleeding, and history of hypertension, diabetes, and myocardial infarction. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same list of predictors with equivalent certainty and phrasing.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The F2 score of logistic regression for predicting in-hospital mortality was 0.81 in the training set, 0.6 in the test set, and 0.59 in the validation data set. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The F2 score of logistic regression in the training set, test set, and validation dataset was 0.81, 0.6, and 0.59, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Identical numeric results and datasets are reported in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The area under the receiver operating characteristic curve (AUC) for logistic regression was 0.77 in the training set, 0.78 in the test set, and 0.8 in the validation data set. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The AUC of logistic regression in the training set, test set, and validation dataset was 0.77, 0.78, and 0.8, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The diagnostic model built by logistic regression was determined to be the best for predicting in-hospital mortality in acute STEMI patients. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Artificial intelligence methods were successfully developed and externally validated for the diagnostic model of in-hospital mortality in acute STEMI patients. (certainty=high, type=contribution)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A machine learning model was trained on 205,571 electronic health records to identify patients with classic symptoms of acute hepatic porphyria (AHP). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A machine learning model was trained on 205,571 electronic health records to identify 22 undiagnosed patients with classic symptoms of acute hepatic porphyria. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model identified 22 patients with classic symptoms of AHP who had neither been diagnosed nor tested for the condition. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A machine learning model was trained on 205,571 electronic health records to identify 22 undiagnosed patients with classic symptoms of acute hepatic porphyria. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Of the 7 patients who agreed to urine porphobilinogen testing, none tested positive for AHP. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: None of the 7 patients who agreed to urine porphobilinogen testing were positive for acute hepatic porphyria. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study explores reasons for the negative testing results and provides lessons learned for future evaluations of machine learning in detecting AHP and other rare diseases. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study provides lessons learned for further work evaluating machine learning to detect acute hepatic porphyria and other rare diseases. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A deep transfer learning-based model named DeQueezeNet is proposed to classify COVID-19 cases using chest X-rays or CT scan images. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A deep transfer learning-based model named DeQueezeNet is proposed to classify COVID-19 cases using chest X-rays or CT scan images. (certainty=high, type=contribution)

alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed model can extract the importance of various influential features from the X-ray images to classify COVID-19 cases. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The performance study of the proposed model demonstrates its effectiveness in terms of accuracy and precision. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The performance study of the proposed model demonstrates its effectiveness in terms of accuracy and precision. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.pub_claim_text: The performance of the proposed model is significantly better compared to recently published works. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Advancing pubertal development by one pubertal stage is associated with a 2.4 months higher brain age across time points. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A one unit increase in pubertal maturational level is associated with a 2.22 months higher brain age across time points. (certainty=high, type=result)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Higher rates of pubertal development are associated with larger positive changes in brain age over time. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Annualized change in pubertal development is weakly related to the rate of change in brain age. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication downgrades the strength of the association (describing it as weak and medium certainty), narrowing and tempering the original stronger claim about rates being associated with larger positive changes.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Brain age prediction provided highly accurate and reliable estimates of individual age, with an overall mean absolute error of 0.7 and 1.4 years at the two timepoints respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Brain age prediction provides highly accurate and reliable estimates of individual age. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the claim of high accuracy and reliability but omits the specific MAE numbers presented in the preprint, so the core claim is preserved with less numerical detail.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The results demonstrate a link between sexual development and brain maturation in early adolescence. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: There is a link between sexual development and brain maturation in early adolescence. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the preprint's primary conclusion about the link between sexual development and brain maturation with equivalent certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: An accurate Deep Neural Network was trained to compute Biological Age based on 36 circulating biomarkers in an Italian population. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: An accurate Deep Neural Network was trained to compute Biological Age based on 36 circulating biomarkers in an Italian population. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same methodological/result statement without substantive change in wording or implication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The resulting Δage (BA-CA) significantly predicted mortality and hospitalization risk for all and specific causes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The resulting Δage (BA–CA) significantly predicted mortality and hospitalization risk for all and specific causes. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication maintains the same predictive result and scope for mortality and hospitalization risk.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Slowed biological aging (Δage<0) was associated with higher physical and mental wellbeing, healthy lifestyles, and higher socioeconomic status. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Slowed biological aging (Δage < 0) was associated with higher physical and mental wellbeing, healthy lifestyles, and higher socioeconomic status. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The association statement is reproduced verbatim in the publication with equivalent certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Accelerated aging (Δage>0) was associated with smoking and obesity. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Accelerated aging (Δage > 0) was associated with smoking and obesity. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the same conclusion linking accelerated aging to smoking and obesity.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Lifestyles and socioeconomic variables explained approximately 48% of the total variance in Δage. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Lifestyles and socioeconomic variables explained approximately 48% of the total variance in Δage, potentially suggesting the existence of a genetic basis. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Bias remains incompletely measured in many medical AI applications. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We found inconsistent instances of model-level bias in the prediction models. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reports observed inconsistent biases in their models (a specific empirical finding) rather than the broader generalization that bias is incompletely measured across many applications.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Current bias evaluation studies may lack a full depiction of the variable effects of a model on its subpopulations. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Current bias evaluation studies may lack a full depiction of the variable effects of a model on its subpopulations. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: This critique is reproduced verbatim in the publication with the same level of certainty and role.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A model can be biased against certain protected groups while being biased towards another protected group. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We found inconsistent instances of model-level bias in the prediction models. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication's note of inconsistent model-level biases reflects the preprint's point that bias can vary in direction across groups, though the wording is less explicit about protected-group directions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A holistic evaluation and diligent search for unrecognized bias are necessary for an unbiased judgment of AI bias. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A holistic evaluation and diligent search for unrecognized bias are necessary for an unbiased judgment of AI bias. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The recommendation for holistic evaluation appears identically in the publication with the same emphasis.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Health Equity Across the AI Lifecycle (HEAAL) framework was developed to evaluate the potential impact of implementing an AI solution on health equity. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Health Equity Across the AI Lifecycle (HEAAL) framework was developed to evaluate the potential impact of implementing an AI solution on health equity. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The contribution statement about HEAAL's development is carried forward unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: HEAAL assesses five equity assessment domains, including accountability, fairness, fitness for purpose, reliability and validity, and transparency. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: HEAAL assesses five equity assessment domains across eight key decision points in the AI adoption lifecycle. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds that those five domains are considered across eight decision points, clarifying and extending the framework's application scope without changing the domains themselves.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The framework contains 37 procedures for evaluating existing AI solutions and 34 procedures for evaluating new AI solutions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The framework contains 37 procedures for evaluating existing AI solutions and 34 procedures for evaluating new AI solutions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The procedural counts are reproduced exactly in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: HEAAL guides healthcare delivery organizations in mitigating the potential risk of AI solutions worsening health inequities. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: HEAAL guides healthcare delivery organizations in mitigating the potential risk of AI solutions worsening health inequities. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The guidance/conclusion about HEAAL's intended use is maintained in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: MMPK-SciML allows for accurate drug plasma concentration predictions for test patients. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: MMPK-SciML provides accurate drug plasma concentration predictions for test patients, whereas classical ML models do not describe the data sufficiently. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication explicitly affirms the accurate prediction capability for test patients and pairs it with a contrast to classical ML models, consistent with the preprint result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: MMPK-SciML predicts drug plasma concentrations more accurately than PopPK models for 5FU. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The MMPK-SciML approach predicts drug plasma concentrations for 5FU more accurately than PopPK models. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the direct comparison for 5FU with the same conclusion and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Classical machine learning models were not able to describe the data sufficiently. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: MMPK-SciML provides accurate drug plasma concentration predictions for test patients, whereas classical ML models do not describe the data sufficiently. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication explicitly notes the insufficiency of classical ML models, matching the preprint's result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: MMPK-SciML should be further investigated as a valuable alternative to classical PopPK modeling. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: MMPK-SciML has shown promising results and should be further investigated as a valuable alternative to classical PopPK modeling, provided there is sufficient training data. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the recommendation to further investigate MMPK-SciML but adds the caveat that sufficient training data are required, narrowing applicability and tempering the claim.

alignments.pub_claim_text: MMPK-SciML allows for the estimation of PopPK parameters and their inter-individual variability using multimodal covariate data without requiring assumptions about the underlying covariate relationships. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: This methodological contribution (estimation of PopPK parameters and variability with multimodal covariates) is present in the publication but was not stated in the preprint.

alignments.pub_claim_text: For sunitinib, MMPK-SciML shows slightly less accurate drug concentration predictions compared to PopPK models. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The sunitinib-specific comparative result appears in the publication but was not reported in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A deep learning model was developed to analyze volumetric growth in patients with medically managed acute uncomplicated type B aortic dissection. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The trained automatic machine learning aortic zone segmentation model demonstrates concordance to the manually segmented aortas. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both statements describe the trained automated segmentation model; the publication emphasizes concordance to manual segmentation which aligns with the development contribution.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Volumetric growth was most pronounced in zones 5 (24%), 4 (13%), and 3 (11%). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report these zone-specific volumetric growth percentages; that specific result from the preprint is omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The trained model demonstrates high concordance to the manually segmented aortas with the strongest performance in zones 4, 5, and 9. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model shows the strongest performance in zones 5 and 9. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains concordance/performance claims but drops zone 4 from the strongest-performance list, narrowing the zones where peak performance is claimed.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: In our limited sample, there were no differences in baseline aortic zone volumes between rapid growth and no/slow growth patients. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: In the limited sample, there were no differences in baseline aortic zone volumes between patients with rapid growth and patients with no or slow growth. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves this result/conclusion about baseline volumes with equivalent wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A trained, automated, open-source aortic zone segmentation model can accurately track changes in aortic growth by zone over time. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The trained automatic machine learning aortic zone segmentation model demonstrates concordance to the manually segmented aortas. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication presents concordance to manual segmentation but does not explicitly claim accurate longitudinal tracking by zone; the claim is therefore toned down and narrowed in scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: GPT-3 answered 62 questions correctly resulting in a failing ISCD score of 289. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: GPT-3 answered 62 questions correctly resulting in a failing ISCD score of 289. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The wording and numerical result are identical between preprint and publication, so the claim is unchanged in content, scope, and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: GPT-4 answered 82 questions correctly with a passing score of 342. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: GPT-4 answered 82 questions correctly with a passing score of 342. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Exact match in numbers and wording; no substantive change in scope or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: GPT-4 scored well above 80% accuracy on all categories except 'Imaging Technology in Bone Health' (65% correct). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: GPT-4 scored well above 80% accuracy on all categories except 'Imaging Technology in Bone Health' (65% correct). (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The substantive data are identical but the reported certainty is lowered in the publication, so content is unchanged while confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: GPT-4 had no responses categorized as nonsensical or totally wrong, unlike GPT-3 which had 23 such responses. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: GPT-4 had no responses categorized as nonsensical or totally wrong, while GPT-3 had 23 such responses. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The claim content and certainty are the same; wording is equivalent and conveys the same comparison.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: GPT algorithms may prove to be valuable physician aids in the diagnoses and monitoring of osteoporosis and other diseases. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: GPT algorithms may prove to be valuable physician aids in the diagnoses and monitoring of osteoporosis and other diseases. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Conclusion language and confidence remain the same between versions, so the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The deep learning models demonstrated an accuracy of 84% at low magnification and 89% at high magnification in predicting the Nugent score categories. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Deep learning models demonstrated 84% accuracy at 400× magnification and 89% at 1,000× magnification. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Same accuracy results; publication adds specific magnifications but does not change the claim substance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: After optimization, the high magnification model achieved 94% accuracy, surpassing the average 92% accuracy of the technicians. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The advanced model achieved 94% accuracy after optimization, outperforming the average 92% accuracy of the technicians. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Numbers and comparison to technicians are identical; wording differs slightly but meaning is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The agreement between deep learning model predictions and technician annotations was 92% for normal vaginal flora, 100% for absence of vaginal flora, 91% for altered vaginal flora, and 100% for bacterial vaginosis. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The agreement between the advanced model predictions and technicians was 92% for normal vaginal flora, 100% for no vaginal flora, 91% for altered vaginal flora, and 100% for bacterial vaginosis. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Agreement percentages match exactly and the claim meaning is preserved; minor wording differences do not alter the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The deep learning models demonstrated accuracy comparable to that of laboratory technicians, which indicates their potential utility in improving the diagnostic accuracy of bacterial vaginosis. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Deep learning models have the potential to diagnose bacterial vaginosis with an accuracy comparable to that of laboratory technicians. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Both state comparable accuracy and potential diagnostic utility; rephrasing does not change substance or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The deep learning models developed in this study predict the Nugent score with high accuracy, thus they can be used to standardize the diagnosis of bacterial vaginosis. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Deep learning models can facilitate reliable diagnoses, even in settings where experienced personnel are scarce. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication shifts emphasis from 'standardizing diagnosis' to enabling reliable diagnoses in resource-limited settings; this changes the claim's framing and broadens its application.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study demonstrated no false negatives and identified true positives and uniquely identified true negatives in predicting recurrence of ductal carcinoma in situ. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study found no false-negatives and identified true-positives and true-negatives in predicting recurrent and nonrecurrent ductal carcinoma in situ. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Core result (no false negatives, identification of true positives/negatives) is the same; publication slightly rephrases but maintains the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Roughly 20% of the microscope fields of recurrent lesions were computationally recurrent. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Roughly 20% of the microscope fields of recurrent lesions were computationally recurrent. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Identical numeric result and certainty; no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The machine tool developed in this study provides the first means to accurately predict recurrent and non-recurrent patient outcomes. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This machine tool provides the first means to accurately predict recurrent and nonrecurrent patient outcomes. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Claim of novelty and predictive capability is verbatim and unchanged in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The intracellular locations of phospho-Ser226-GLUT1 and phosphofructokinase type L likely participate in cancer recurrences by accelerating glucose flux. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The intracellular locations of phospho-Ser226-glucose transporter type 1 and phosphofructokinase type L likely participate in cancer recurrences by accelerating glucose flux. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The mechanistic conclusion is preserved but the publication lowers the stated certainty, so the claim content is unchanged while confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: TzanckNet achieved an accuracy of 94.3% in identifying cell types in Tzanck smear test findings. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: TzanckNet achieved an accuracy of 94.3% in identifying cell types in Tzanck smear test findings. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Exact match in performance metric and wording; no change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The sensitivity of TzanckNet was found to be 83.7%, while the specificity was 97.3%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The sensitivity of TzanckNet was 83.7% and the specificity was 97.3% for the identification of six cell types. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds the detail that these metrics apply to identification of six cell types, clarifying the context without changing the measured performance or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: TzanckNet has the potential to lower the experience barrier needed to use the Tzanck smear test. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: TzanckNet has the potential to lower the experience barrier needed to use the Tzanck smear test. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Conclusion and certainty are the same in both versions; no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The area under the receiver operating characteristic curve for TzanckNet was 0.974. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The area under the receiver operating characteristic curve for TzanckNet was 0.974. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Identical numeric performance metric and certainty; unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SymScore produces score tables for shortened questionnaires that enable clinicians to estimate results reflecting those of the original questionnaires. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: SymScore produces score tables for shortened questionnaires that enable clinicians to estimate results reflecting those of the original questionnaires. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Identical claim and certainty; no change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SymScore demonstrated comparable performance to the MCQI-6 and achieved AU-ROC values closely matching those of SLEEPS. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: SymScore's performance is comparable to that of the MCQI-6 and SLEEPS questionnaires in assessing sleep disorders. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Performance comparison is preserved; publication rephrases but does not alter the substance or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SymScore ensures that healthcare professionals can easily explain and trust its results without specialized machine learning knowledge. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: SymScore ensures that healthcare professionals can easily explain and trust its results without specialized machine learning knowledge. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Conclusion and confidence remain the same; wording identical.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SymScore advances explainable AI for healthcare by offering a user-friendly and resource-efficient alternative to machine learning-based questionnaires. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: SymScore advances explainable AI for healthcare by offering a user-friendly and resource-efficient alternative to machine learning-based questionnaires. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Claim about contribution and its certainty are unchanged between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Logistic Regression was the best modeling approach for predicting Hospital Acquired Pressure Injuries with an AUC of 0.91±0.034.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Logistic regression achieved a performance (AUC = 0.91 ± 0.034) comparable to the other machine learning approaches.
alignments.rationale: The preprint states LR was the best; the publication retains the same AUC but reframes it as comparable to other ML methods, weakening the original 'best' claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There is discordance between predictors deemed important by machine learning models compared to traditional statistical models.
alignments.pub_claim_text: There is discordance between machine learning derived predictors compared to the traditional statistical model.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same observation about discordant predictors with essentially identical wording and meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning models should be compared to traditional modeling approaches to ensure proper utilization in clinical decision making.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning models will continue to inform clinical decision-making processes but should be compared to traditional modeling approaches to ensure proper utilization.
alignments.rationale: The publication keeps the core recommendation to compare ML with traditional models but adds context that ML will continue to inform decision-making, clarifying rather than changing the recommendation.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Disagreements between important predictors found by traditional and machine learning modeling approaches can potentially confuse clinicians.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Disagreements between important predictors found by traditional and machine learning modeling approaches can potentially confuse clinicians and need to be reconciled.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the potential for clinician confusion and adds that such disagreements 'need to be reconciled', increasing the urgency (amplified confidence) of the concern.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Future efforts to analyze time-stamped, prospective medical record data will be enhanced by patient-specific details.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not reiterate this specific forward-looking point about patient-specific, time-stamped data; instead it adds a different contribution about EMR integration, so the preprint claim is not present.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Standard models for COVID-19 prediction have shown low accuracy for long-term prediction due to high uncertainty and lack of essential data.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Standard models for COVID-19 prediction have shown low accuracy for long-term prediction due to high uncertainty and lack of essential data.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this limitation verbatim, so the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The essential generalization and robustness abilities of existing COVID-19 prediction models need to be improved.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The paper provides an initial benchmarking to demonstrate the potential of machine learning for future research in outbreak prediction.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not explicitly restate a strong need to improve generalization/robustness but frames the work as an initial benchmarking for future research, which softens and repositions the original claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Two machine learning models, multi-layered perceptron (MLP) and adaptive network-based fuzzy inference system (ANFIS), showed promising results in predicting the COVID-19 outbreak.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Two machine learning models, multi-layered perceptron (MLP) and adaptive network-based fuzzy inference system (ANFIS), showed promising results in predicting the COVID-19 outbreak.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this result directly; there is no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning is suggested as an effective tool to model the COVID-19 outbreak due to its highly complex nature and variation in behavior from nation-to-nation.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning is suggested as an effective tool to model the COVID-19 outbreak due to its highly complex nature and variation across nations.
alignments.rationale: The publication expresses the same conclusion with slight wording change ('across nations'), preserving meaning and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The XGBoost model achieved the best performance with an AUROC of 0.94.
alignments.pub_claim_text: XGBoost demonstrated the best performance in predicting low birth weight, achieving an AUROC of 0.94.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the result identically, so the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Catboost and Random Forest also showed excellent results, confirming the effectiveness of these models in predicting LBW.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report or highlight Catboost/Random Forest results in the provided claims, so this preprint claim appears omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning, combined with SMOTE, proved to be an effective approach for predicting LBW.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning, combined with SMOTE, proved to be an effective approach for predicting low birth weight.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same methodological conclusion without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: These models can be applied to identify high-risk pregnancies, improving perinatal outcomes through early interventions.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The models developed can be applied to identify high-risk pregnancies, improving perinatal outcomes through early interventions.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the application and intended impact identically, so the claim is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The automated de-identification system outperforms existing best-in-class tools with a recall of 0.992 and 0.994 on the I2B2 and Mayo Clinic data, respectively.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The automated de-identification system outperforms existing tools with a recall of 0.992 and precision of 0.979 on the i2b2 2014 dataset.
alignments.rationale: The publication splits and augments reported metrics (adding precision) and presents dataset-specific details; the core performance claim is preserved but the presentation is clarified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The system achieves a precision of 0.979 and 0.967 on the I2B2 and Mayo Clinic data, respectively.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The system achieves a recall of 0.994 and precision of 0.967 on a dataset of 10,000 notes from the Mayo Clinic.
alignments.rationale: The publication provides dataset-level detail and pairs recall/precision for the Mayo dataset; the numeric precision for Mayo matches and overall reporting is clarified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The automated de-identification system can enable the generation of de-identified patient data at the scale required for modern machine learning applications.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The de-identification system enables the generation of de-identified patient data at the scale required for modern machine-learning applications.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates this conclusion directly, so it is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: EchoNet-Labs achieved an area under the curve (AUC) of 0.80 in predicting anemia.
alignments.pub_claim_text: EchoNet-Labs achieved an AUC of 0.80 in detecting anemia on the held-out test set of Stanford patients.
alignments.rationale: The publication specifies the dataset (held-out Stanford set) while preserving the AUC result, clarifying context but not changing the claim's substance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: EchoNet-Labs achieved an AUC of 0.82 in predicting elevated B-type natriuretic peptide (BNP).
alignments.pub_claim_text: EchoNet-Labs achieved an AUC of 0.86 in detecting elevated BNP on the held-out test set of Stanford patients.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports a higher AUC (0.86 vs 0.82) on the held-out Stanford set, changing the numeric result and increasing reported performance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Deep learning applied to diagnostic imaging can provide additional clinical value and identify phenotypic information beyond current imaging interpretation methods.
alignments.pub_claim_text: These results show that deep learning applied to diagnostic imaging can provide additional clinical value and identify phenotypic information beyond current imaging interpretation methods.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates this high-level conclusion directly, so it is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: EchoNet-Labs can predict common biomarker results from echocardiogram videos.
alignments.pub_claim_text: EchoNet-Labs demonstrated the utility in detecting abnormalities in 10 additional lab tests.
alignments.rationale: The preprint's broader claim about biomarkers is made more specific in the publication by naming detection utility for 10 particular lab tests, narrowing the scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model demonstrates utility in predicting abnormalities in 10 additional lab tests.
alignments.pub_claim_text: EchoNet-Labs demonstrated the utility in detecting abnormalities in 10 additional lab tests.
alignments.rationale: This publication claim matches the preprint claim directly and is unchanged in content.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our random forest model was able to distinguish hypertensive from normotensive patients with an accuracy of 84.2%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Random Forest model achieved an accuracy of 84.2% in distinguishing hypertensive from normotensive patients. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The accuracy value and claim are identical between preprint and publication, indicating no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model achieved a sensitivity of 91.4% and a specificity of 66.7%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model demonstrated a specificity of 78.0% and sensitivity of 84.0% for detecting hypertension. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The diagnostic metrics are reported with different numeric values in the publication (sensitivity decreased, specificity increased), so the result has been revised and overall sensitivity is lower (tempered).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Age, body mass index (BMI), and specific ECG-derived features were identified as the most important factors for the model's success. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Age, BMI, and specific ECG-derived features were identified as the most important factors contributing to the model's success. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The factors listed and their role are the same in both versions with no substantive change in framing or certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our machine learning algorithm is effective in detecting hypertension using ECG-derived and basic anthropometric criteria. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The machine learning algorithm effectively detects hypertension using ECG-derived and basic anthropometric criteria. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion about the algorithm's effectiveness is restated without meaningful change in scope or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The findings suggest new opportunities for identifying undiagnosed hypertensive individuals at increased risk for cardiovascular disease. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The findings suggest potential for identifying undiagnosed hypertensive individuals at increased risk for cardiovascular disease. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Wording is slightly altered but the substantive conclusion (potential to identify undiagnosed at-risk individuals) remains the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The analysis of high-quality genomic variant data enables researchers to identify novel biomarkers and stratify patients based on disease risk factors. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The broad claim about genomic-variant analysis enabling biomarker discovery and stratification does not appear explicitly in the publication, which focuses on the VAREANT tool and modules.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: VAREANT is an accessible and configurable bioinformatic tool designed to support the preparation of variant data into a usable analysis-ready format. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: VAREANT is an accessible and configurable bioinformatic application designed to support the preparation of genomic variant data into a usable analysis-ready format. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same contribution claim about VAREANT with equivalent wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: VAREANT has been successfully tested and validated on numerous variable-sized datasets and implemented in two case studies involving patients with CVDs. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: VAREANT has been successfully tested and validated on numerous variable-sized datasets. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication explicitly documents the testing/validation claim; implementation in case studies appears separately in the publication (see Q4), so the original combined claim has been split into distinct published claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Using tools like VAREANT can efficiently extract relevant variants into an AI/ML ready format, producing targeted and high-quality datasets. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: VAREANT includes three standalone modules: Pre-processing, Variant Annotation, and AI/ML Data Preparation. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: A general claim about efficient AI/ML-ready data extraction has been made more concrete in the publication by listing specific modules that implement that capability, narrowing the claim to the tool's structure.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Increasing physical activity might effectively lower depressive symptoms and improve glycemic control in low-income ethnic minority patients with comorbid diabetes and depression. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Increasing physical activity might effectively lower depressive symptoms and improve glycaemic control in low-income ethnic minority patients with comorbid diabetes and depression. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The hypothesis/conclusion is restated verbatim (with minor spelling difference) and retains the same framing and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Tailoring interventions using machine learning methods likely increases the effectiveness of the intervention. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Tailoring interventions using machine learning methods likely increases the effectiveness of the intervention. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The claim about tailored ML-based interventions is unchanged in wording and certainty between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study will compare the effectiveness of an adaptive text-messaging smartphone application to encourage physical activity against a uniform random intervention and a control group. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A three-arm randomised controlled trial will be conducted to examine the effect of a text-messaging smartphone application on physical activity. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the planned comparison as a three-arm RCT (consistent with adaptive vs random vs control), providing a slightly more formal description of the trial design.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The trial aims to recruit 276 adults diagnosed with diabetes and elevated depressive symptoms to assess the intervention's effects over a 6-month follow-up. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The specific recruitment target (276 adults) and enrollment detail do not appear among the publication claims provided.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The smartphone application is a novel approach to target both diabetes and depressive symptoms in a low-income vulnerable patient population. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication claims list does not explicitly state that the app is a novel approach; that characterization is not present in the provided publication claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning methods were able to automatically detect changes in brain activity during ayahuasca consumption. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The machine learning method was able to automatically detect changes in brain activity, with case (B) showing the highest accuracy (92%). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both versions report that ML detected changes in brain activity; the publication adds a note about which case showed highest accuracy but the central result is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The machine learning method showed the highest accuracy (92%) when applied to the correlation of the EEG time series. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The machine learning method was able to automatically detect changes in brain activity, with case (B) showing the highest accuracy (92%). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The 92% accuracy result is preserved in the publication and tied to a specific case; the numeric result is consistent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The correlation between F3 and PO4 was identified as the most important brain connection during ayahuasca-mediated visual hallucinations. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: F3 and PO4 were identified as the most important brain connections, representing a significant new discovery for psychedelic literature. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The same connection is highlighted, but the publication presents it more strongly (framed as a significant new discovery and with higher certainty), thus amplifying the confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Closeness centrality and assortativity were the most important complex network measures associated with ayahuasca consumption. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Closeness centrality and assortativity were the most important complex network measures associated with ayahuasca use. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The specific network measures and their importance are preserved between preprint and publication without meaningful change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The application of machine learning and complex network measurements are useful methods to study the effects of ayahuasca on brain activity and medical use. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: A general methodological claim about usefulness is not explicitly present among the provided publication claims, which focus on specific findings and additional broader claims not in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Early use of the antiemetic agent ondansetron was associated with increased survival in mechanically ventilated COVID-19 patients. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Early use of the antiemetic agent ondansetron was associated with decreased any cause mortality 30 days post-PCR+ testing in mechanically ventilated patients. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the association but clarifies the outcome (decreased any-cause 30-day mortality post-PCR+), making the timing and endpoint more specific.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study employed artificial intelligence analytics on real-world data to identify significant predictors of mortality for specific COVID-19 patient populations. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study identified novel predictors of COVID-19 any cause mortality using artificial intelligence analytics on real-world data. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the contribution and emphasizes novelty of predictors; the core claim about using AI on real-world data to identify predictors is preserved but slightly strengthened in wording.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Real world COVID-19 focused data analysis using artificial intelligence can generate valid insights that could support clinical decision-making. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The findings suggest that real-world COVID-19-focused data analysis using artificial intelligence can generate unexpected yet valid insights. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conclusion is preserved but the publication refines it by noting the insights may be 'unexpected yet valid,' a modest clarification of the original claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Simulations confirmed that our framework adequately controls linkage disequilibrium and can infer nonlinear effects when such effects genuinely exist.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The usefulness of neural-networks for generating polygenic scores may currently be limited and confounded by joint tagging effects due to linkage disequilibrium.
alignments.rationale: The preprint claims successful control of LD and ability to infer nonlinear effects; the publication instead emphasizes limitations and confounding by joint tagging, effectively reversing/limiting the original claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Using our approach on real data, we found evidence for small amounts of nonlinearity due to gene-gene and gene-environment interactions which mildly improved prediction performance by approximately 7% and 4%, respectively.
alignments.pub_claim_text: We found evidence for small amounts of nonlinear effects in the phenotypes analyzed.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the core finding of small nonlinear effects but removes specific interaction types and the reported percent improvements, so the claim is presented more cautiously and with less scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Despite evidence for nonlinear effects, neural network models were outperformed by linear regression models for both genetic-only and genetic+environmental input scenarios.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Neural-network models were outperformed by linear regression models for both genetic-only and genetic+environmental input scenarios.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same result (neural networks were outperformed by linear models) with equivalent scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We found substantial evidence for confounding by joint tagging effects, whereby inferred gene-gene interactions were actually linkage disequilibrium due to unaccounted for additive genetic variants.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The usefulness of neural-networks for generating polygenic scores may currently be limited and confounded by joint tagging effects due to linkage disequilibrium.
alignments.rationale: The publication explicitly mentions confounding by joint tagging/LD as a limitation, matching the preprint's identification of this confounder.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our results indicate that the usefulness of neural networks for generating polygenic scores for common traits and diseases may currently be limited.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The usefulness of neural-networks for generating polygenic scores may currently be limited and confounded by joint tagging effects due to linkage disequilibrium.
alignments.rationale: The publication keeps the conclusion that neural networks may be limited but clarifies the reason by adding confounding by joint tagging/LD.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The gradient boosting algorithm was the best performer in predicting mortality among confirmed CoVID-19 patients, achieving an area under the ROC curve of 0.966.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The logistic regression algorithm was the best performer in predicting mortality among confirmed CoVID-19 patients, achieving an area under the ROC curve of 0.830.
alignments.rationale: The publication changes which algorithm is reported best and reports a substantially lower AUC, indicating a substantive revision of the performance claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The gradient boosting algorithm demonstrated superior calibration with a Matthews Correlation Coefficient of 0.656 and a Brier Score of 0.013.
alignments.rationale: These specific calibration metrics for gradient boosting are not reported in the publication and the best algorithm claim was changed; the detailed metric claim appears omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The accuracy of the gradient boosting algorithm in predicting mortality was 0.987.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report this accuracy value or attribute it to the final reported best model; the numeric accuracy claim is omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: An open-source online prediction tool named CoCoMoRP was developed and deployed for predicting mortality risk among CoVID-19 confirmed patients.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The best performing algorithm, logistic regression, was deployed as an online CoVID-19 Community Mortality Risk Prediction tool named CoCoMoRP.
alignments.rationale: Both versions report development/deployment of CoCoMoRP; the publication clarifies that the deployed tool uses logistic regression (different from the preprint's reported best algorithm).

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The developed tool can be utilized by health providers and policy makers to triage patients at the community level.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The developed tool can be utilized by health providers and policymakers to triage patients at the community level.
alignments.rationale: The recommendation for tool use by providers and policymakers is preserved verbatim in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The hybrid machine learning methods of ANFIS and MLP-ICA predict that by late May, the outbreak and the total mortality will drop substantially.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The models predict that by late May, the outbreak and the total mortality will drop substantially.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the specific prediction about late-May declines, though reported certainty is slightly reduced, so the claim is largely unchanged but somewhat tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The validation of the model is performed for nine days with promising results, confirming the model accuracy.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The validation is performed for 9 days with promising results, which confirms the model accuracy.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the nine-day validation and the conclusion of promising/confirmed accuracy unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This study suggests machine learning as an effective tool to model the COVID-19 outbreak due to its complex nature and variation in behavior from nation-to-nation.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The hybrid machine learning methods of ANFIS and MLP-ICA are proposed to predict time series of infected individuals and mortality rate.
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the statement as a proposal of specific methods rather than a general assertion of ML's effectiveness, reducing scope and expressed confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study provides an initial benchmarking to demonstrate the potential of machine learning for future research.
alignments.pub_claim_text: This paper provides an initial benchmarking to demonstrate the potential of machine learning for future research.
alignments.rationale: The publication keeps the benchmarking claim and assigns higher certainty, slightly amplifying the contribution statement.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning models based on billing codes alone can predict preterm birth risk at various gestational ages with a ROC-AUC of 0.75 and PR-AUC of 0.40 at 28 weeks of gestation.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning models based on billing codes alone can predict preterm birth risk at various gestational ages with a ROC-AUC of 0.75 at 28 weeks of gestation.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the ROC-AUC result but omits the PR-AUC value, so the core claim is preserved but with less detail.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The machine learning models outperform comparable models trained using known risk factors, which have a ROC-AUC of 0.65 and PR-AUC of 0.25 at 28 weeks.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The machine learning models outperform comparable models trained using known risk factors, achieving a ROC-AUC of 0.65 at 28 weeks.
alignments.rationale: The publication keeps the comparative performance claim but omits the PR-AUC, reducing reported detail while preserving the main result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model stratifies deliveries into interpretable groups, including high-risk preterm birth sub-types enriched for distinct comorbidities.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The machine learning approach predicts preterm birth subtypes, mode of delivery, and recurrent preterm birth.
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the output more broadly (subtypes, mode, recurrence) compared to the preprint's emphasis on interpretable, comorbidity-enriched groups, expanding the range of predicted outcomes.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The prediction models maintain their accuracy on a large, independent cohort from a different healthcare system.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The prediction models maintain their accuracy on a large, independent cohort from a different healthcare system.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the external validation result equivalently.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Machine learning algorithms have great potential to improve medical care during pregnancy by leveraging rich phenotypic and genetic features derived from EHRs.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning algorithms have great potential to improve medical care during pregnancy, but further work is needed before clinical application.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the potential claim but adds a caution that further work is required, tempering the original optimism.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The developed deep learning model achieved an AUROC of 0.936 during external validation on a large Chinese dataset.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our model showed excellent performance with an AUROC of 0.930 in the external geographic validation set.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports essentially the same external validation result with a slightly different AUROC value and similar framing of performance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The sensitivity, specificity, negative predictive value, and positive predictive value of the model were 0.753, 0.909, 0.711, and 0.925, respectively, at a probability threshold of 0.5.
alignments.rationale: These specific thresholded performance metrics are not presented in the publication; the paper instead reports calibration and other performance summaries, so the detailed numbers are omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Deep learning can reduce stress on healthcare systems by automatically screening CT imaging for COVID-19.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Deep learning can reduce stress on healthcare systems by automatically screening CT imaging for COVID-19.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same conclusion about potential system-level benefits of automated CT screening.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Deep learning models must be robustly assessed using various performance measures and externally validated in each setting.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Best practice guidelines for developing and reporting predictive models are vital for the safe adoption of such models.
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the call for robust assessment and validation into a broader recommendation for best-practice guidelines, preserving the safety/rigor emphasis but altering wording.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A novel method to analyze volumetric imaging data composed of slices, such as CT images, was developed using deep learning.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our method showed improved generalizability in external validation compared to previously published methods.
alignments.rationale: The publication asserts improved generalizability of the presented method (an implicit claim about the novel method's value), which aligns with but reframes the preprint's contribution statement about a novel volumetric analysis method.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A multitude of machine learning algorithms were applied to refine the early prediction of sepsis. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A multitude of machine learning algorithms were applied to refine the early prediction of sepsis. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Text is essentially identical between preprint and publication, conveying the same result without change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The quality of the studies ranged from 'poor' to 'very good' based on the quality criteria satisfied. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The quality of the studies ranged from 'poor' to 'very good' based on the quality criteria satisfied. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Claim wording and meaning are unchanged between versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The massive inter-study heterogeneity in model development and definitions precluded a meta-analysis. (certainty=high, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The massive inter-study heterogeneity in model development and definitions precluded a meta-analysis. (certainty=high, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The limitation is stated identically in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Only 2 studies provided publicly-accessible source code and data sources, which fosters reproducibility. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Only two studies provided publicly accessible source code and data sources fostering reproducibility. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: Same fact reported with only minor orthographic differences (numeric wording), meaning unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This review highlights several shortcomings of current approaches, including low comparability and reproducibility. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This review highlights several shortcomings of the current approaches, including low comparability and reproducibility. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Conclusion language and content are effectively identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A natural language processing pipeline was developed to identify risk factors in clinical text and calculate risk for ischaemic stroke and bleeding. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A natural language processing pipeline was developed to identify risk factors in clinical text and calculate risk for ischaemic stroke and bleeding. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The contribution is described identically in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Automatic risk scores were in strong agreement with the two independent experts for CHA 2 DS 2 -VASc, with an average kappa of 0.78. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Automatic risk scores were in strong agreement with the two independent experts for CHA2DS2-VASc, with an average kappa of 0.78. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Same numeric result and interpretation; only minor formatting differences in score name.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: In high-risk patients, oral anticoagulation use has increased significantly over the last 7 years, driven by the availability of DOACs. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: In high-risk patients (CHA2DS2-VASc ≥2), oral anticoagulation use has increased significantly over the last 7 years. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Publication specifies the operational definition of "high-risk" (CHA2DS2-VASc ≥2) and omits the preprint's causal attribution to DOAC availability, changing and narrowing the claim and reducing causal certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Factors independently associated with oral anticoagulation use included components of the CHA 2 DS 2 -VASc and HAS-BLED scores, as well as discharging specialty and frailty. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Factors independently associated with oral anticoagulation use included components of the CHA2DS2-VASc and HAS-BLED scores, discharging specialty, and frailty. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Variables listed and the nature of the conclusion are the same; only minor formatting changes.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Electronic health record text can be used for automatic calculation of clinical risk scores at scale. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Analysis of routinely collected electronic health record data can replicate findings from large-scale curated registries. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Publication reframes the preprint's method-focused claim as a broader statement about EHR analyses replicating registry findings; the idea is related but meaning is changed and broader.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The SMH-TB database contains retrospective patient data spanning 2011 to 2018 for a total of 3298 patients. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The SMH-TB database contains retrospective patient data spanning 2011 to 2018 for a total of 3298 patients. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Identical factual statement across versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The performance of TB diagnosis and medication NLP rulesets surpasses 93% in recall, precision, and F 1 metrics, indicating good generalizability. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The performance of TB diagnosis and medication NLP rulesets surpasses 93% in recall, precision, and F1 metrics, indicating good generalizability. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Same performance claim with only formatting normalization.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: An estimated 20% of patients were diagnosed with active TB and 46% with latent TB, with adjustments for potential misclassification resulting in 18% and 40% respectively. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: An estimated 20% of patients were diagnosed with active TB and 46% with latent TB, with adjustments for potential misclassification resulting in 18% and 40% respectively. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Percentages and interpretation are identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: SMH-TB is a unique database that includes a breadth of structured data derived from structured and unstructured EHR data. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The SMH-TB database is a unique resource that includes structured data derived from both structured and unstructured EHR data using NLP rulesets. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Publication clarifies that structured data were derived using NLP rulesets from unstructured EHR text; meaning is the same but with added methodological detail.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The NLP algorithm had 100% accuracy for identifying 4 SIRVA cases and 96 individuals without SIRVA in the validation sample. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The NLP algorithm achieved 100% accuracy for identifying 4 SIRVA cases and 96 cases without SIRVA in the validation sample. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Claim is effectively identical; only minor wording change ('individuals'->'cases').

alignments.preprint_claim_text: In a broader cohort of 53,585 individuals, the NLP algorithm identified 291 definite, 124 probable, and 52 possible SIRVA cases. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: In a broader cohort of 53,585 vaccinations, the NLP algorithm identified 291 definite, 124 probable, and 52 possible SIRVA cases. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Counts and case-classification are unchanged; wording shifted from 'individuals' to 'vaccinations' but meaning is preserved.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The chart-confirmation rates for the identified SIRVA cases were 95.3% for definite, 67.7% for probable, and 18.9% for possible cases. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The chart-confirmation rates for the identified SIRVA cases were 95.5% for definite, 67.7% for probable, and 17.3% for possible cases. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Publication reports slightly adjusted confirmation percentages (minor rounding/data update), which clarifies the precise values without changing the conclusion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The algorithm performed with high sensitivity and reasonable specificity in identifying positive SIRVA cases. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The algorithm performed with high sensitivity and reasonable specificity in identifying positive SIRVA cases. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Conclusion about algorithm performance is consistent between versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The NLP algorithm can potentially be used in future population-based studies to identify this rare adverse event, avoiding labor-intensive chart review validation. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The NLP algorithm can potentially be used in future population-based studies to identify SIRVA, avoiding labor-intensive chart review validation. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The prospective application and benefit (reducing chart review) is stated similarly in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed semi-supervised deep learning method for detecting and classifying breast nodules achieves results comparable to supervised training networks. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The performance of the semi-supervised model was comparable to that of the supervised learning model trained on a larger dataset of 5,196 labeled nodules. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Publication retains the comparability claim but adds context (comparison to a supervised model trained on a larger labeled dataset), clarifying the original statement.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The AUC for semi-supervised learning is 93.7% and 92% on two test data sets, which is similar to the AUC of 94.2% and 92.3% for supervised learning. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The semi-supervised model outperformed the supervised learning model when trained with 800 labeled and 4,396 unlabeled nodules, achieving an area under the curve (AUC) of 0.934±0.026 compared to 0.83±0.050. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Publication presents different performance numbers and a stronger claim of outperformance in a specific low-label training regime, which revises and narrows the original AUC comparison and amplifies the positive result.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Semi-supervised learning strategies have good application potential in medical images. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The semi-supervised model reduces the number of labeled images required for training, alleviating the difficulty in data preparation for medical artificial intelligence. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Publication gives a concrete advantage (reducing labeled images) that clarifies and supports the general application potential claim from the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The results indicate that semi-supervised learning can achieve similar performance to supervised deep learning with fewer labeled samples. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The performance of the semi-supervised model was comparable to that of the supervised learning model trained on a larger dataset of 5,196 labeled nodules. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Publication explicitly states similar performance when compared against a supervised model trained on more labeled data, matching the preprint's conclusion about fewer labeled samples.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The DL model, ResNet18 trained from balanced, pre-augmented PD images, achieved high accuracy in classifying the groups with an overall F1-score of 96.8%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The DL model, ResNet18 trained from balanced, pre-augmented PD images, achieved high accuracy in classifying the groups with an overall F1-score of 96.8%. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the exact result and metric with no change in wording or emphasis.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The DL models successfully localized the affected VF loss in PD plots. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The DL models successfully localized the affected VF loss in PD plots. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The claim is reproduced verbatim in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The evidence-based DL model trained from PD images demonstrated that the DL model could stage glaucoma using only PD plots like Mills criteria. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The evidence-based DL model trained from PD images demonstrated that the DL model could stage glaucoma using only PD plots like Mills criteria. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication contains the same conclusion with no substantive change.
